title: Coelophysis — overview
text: A slender, lightly built coelophysoid theropod from the Late Triassic of New Mexico, Coelophysis was a fast, bipedal carnivore around 3 metres long and one of the earliest dinosaurs known from abundant material. Hundreds of individuals preserved together at Ghost Ranch allow study of this species across age classes and in population-scale detail unusual for any Triassic animal. A population, not a specimen: Most early dinosaurs are known from a single broken skeleton; the Ghost Ranch quarry yielded hundreds together , so we can measure variation, growth and sex the way biologists study a herd of living deer. The blueprint arrived early: Hollow bones and long shins mark a committed sprinter - strikingly, the theropod body plan was already near-complete here, and would barely change in concept across the 150 million years leading to Tyrannosaurus. A cannibal myth, corrected: Small skeletons inside adult ribcages looked like cannibalism until re-study showed they were swallowed crocodile-relatives - a clean example of a vivid story collapsing under a second look. One species wearing two builds: 'Gracile' and 'robust' forms once tempted a split into two species; they are now read as intraspecific variation - possibly sexual dimorphism, possibly growth-related differences - no longer a reason to split the species. Coelophysis was a lightly built pursuit predator that ran down insects, small reptiles and early mammal relatives across a seasonally arid floodplain, snapping with a narrow, finely serrated jaw rather than grappling. In its ecosystem it filled the small-agile-hunter niche that later dinosaurs would hand to dromaeosaurs like Velociraptor, while larger crocodile-line predators of the Triassic took the role of top carnivore. The Ghost Ranch mass death may record a single drought or flood, which keeps alive the open question of whether these animals truly moved as a group - a crowd in death is not proof of a herd in life. Coelophysis sits near the base of Theropoda , the lineage running through Allosaurus and Tyrannosaurus to living birds. It shows the predatory dinosaur 'recipe' - bipedal, hollow-boned, fast - was essentially fixed almost the moment dinosaurs appeared. Because we have so many individuals, Coelophysis turns 'what did this animal look like' into 'how did it vary, grow and differ by sex' - the shift from describing a fossil to studying a population is one of the quiet revolutions of modern paleontology.

title: Coelophysis — fossil record
text: Hundreds of associated skeletons from the Ghost Ranch mass-death quarry, New Mexico; additional material from the Coelophysis Quarry at Petrified Forest, Arizona Evidence level: Rich. Ghost Ranch, New Mexico; Petrified Forest, Arizona, USA Chinle Formation Material: Multiple skeletons, Bonebed, Skull, Limb bones, Vertebrae. The Ghost Ranch site (Whitaker Quarry, discovered 1947) yielded well over a thousand individual bones representing dozens to hundreds of individuals - an extraordinary concentration that allows population-level study of growth, sex ratios, and individual variation in a way almost unmatched in the Triassic. Bone microstructure confirms relatively rapid growth. The sheer number of specimens gives Coelophysis one of the best-documented anatomies of any early dinosaur, and the assemblage itself is a geological puzzle: whether the animals died together or were transported to one place by a flood is still argued. The 'cannibalism' episode visible in some specimens has been re-examined and reinterpreted as swallowed crocodile-relatives, illustrating how even apparently clear fossil evidence can be reversed.

title: Coelophysis — Did Coelophysis live and hunt in groups?
text: Did Coelophysis live and hunt in groups? Mass death assemblages can form without social behaviour, so the bonebed alone can't settle it. Social packs (moderate confidence): A concentration of one lightly built pursuit predator hints at tolerant grouping or cooperation. Drought trap (moderate confidence): Animals may have crowded a shrinking waterhole independently and died together by chance.

title: Eoraptor — overview
text: A small, early dinosaur from the Late Triassic Ischigualasto Formation of Argentina, Eoraptor is among the oldest members of the group and dates to within a few million years of the dinosaur lineage's origin. At roughly 1 metre long, it retains a generalist body plan - currently classified as a basal sauropodomorph, though its exact position is still debated - that sits close to the common ancestor of the great sauropod and theropod lineages. Two diets in one jaw: It carries pointed meat-eating teeth and leaf-shaped plant teeth together - anatomy frozen at an evolutionary fork, before its lineage had chosen a side. Reclassified by its mouth: First called a primitive theropod, its teeth and skull moved it onto the sauropodomorph line - meaning this 10 kg omnivore is an ancestor-grade relative of the 70-tonne Argentinosaurus. Hands that could seize: A partly opposable thumb on a five-fingered hand suggests it could grab varied food , the hallmark of a generalist rather than a specialist. A bit-player at the start: It comes from one of the earliest dinosaur communities , where dinosaurs were minor figures among more numerous crocodile-relatives - dominance was earned later, not given. Eoraptor was a small, quick biped that ate whatever it could crop or catch, its mixed dentition pointing to a flexible opportunist . Crucially, it was not the ruler of its world: it shared ground with crocodile-line reptiles and mammal forerunners that were, at the time, just as successful, so it likely avoided competition by staying small, fast and unfussy while larger archosaurs dominated. That ecological modesty is the point - dinosaurs began at the margins and only inherited the planet after later extinctions cleared their rivals. Eoraptor shows the sauropodomorph lineage beginning as a tiny omnivore - the same branch that produced the largest land animals ever. The road from this animal to Argentinosaurus is among the most extreme size transformations in the history of life. Eoraptor is a snapshot of dinosaurs before they were special - small, flexible and outnumbered. It quietly dismantles the assumption that evolutionary dominance is present from the start; far more often, it is a late inheritance.

title: Eoraptor — fossil record
text: Several nearly complete skulls and partial skeletons from the Ischigualasto Formation Evidence level: Rich. Ischigualasto Provincial Park, San Juan, Argentina Ischigualasto Formation Material: Skull, Partial skeleton, Teeth, Limb bones. Eoraptor is represented by multiple specimens including at least two well-preserved skulls, giving palaeontologists detailed anatomical information for such an early dinosaur. Its teeth and skull anatomy have been scrutinised closely because they bear directly on whether it belongs to the sauropodomorph or theropod line - a debate that illustrates how even good fossil material can leave phylogenetic placement genuinely uncertain. The Ischigualasto Formation is one of the world's key Triassic dinosaur-bearing units, and its fossils come from a time-stamped volcanic context that allows reliable radiometric dating.

title: Eoraptor — Was Eoraptor an early meat-eater or an early plant-eater's ancestor?
text: Was Eoraptor an early meat-eater or an early plant-eater's ancestor? Its transitional teeth genuinely straddle the diets of two major lineages. Basal sauropodomorph (strong confidence): Skull and dental details align it with the long-neck lineage; the leading view. Basal theropod (low confidence): The original interpretation, kept by a minority stressing its predatory traits.

title: Herrerasaurus — overview
text: A large, early predatory saurischian from the Late Triassic Ischigualasto Formation of Argentina, Herrerasaurus reached around 6 metres at a time when most dinosaurs were still small, making it one of the earliest known large predators within the group. Its exact position in the dinosaur family tree has been long debated, with some analyses placing it near the base of the theropod line and others at a deeper node in the saurischian tree. Big before dinosaurs ruled: While Eoraptor shared its world at house-cat size, Herrerasaurus reached 6 metres - proof that large body size evolved in dinosaurs almost immediately, not only after they took over. A flexible, sliding jaw: A hinge midway along its lower jaw let it grip struggling prey , an early experiment in the kill mechanics theropods would refine for the next 160 million years. Hard to place on the tree: Its mix of features has had scientists debating whether it's a very early theropod or a saurischian outsider - a living reminder that the base of the dinosaur tree is still being untangled. Outranked despite its size: It hunted alongside, and was likely outcompeted by, large crocodile-line predators - being a big dinosaur in the Triassic was no guarantee of being top predator. Herrerasaurus was the leading dinosaurian predator of the Ischigualasto fauna, taking small reptiles and early plant-eaters with a fast bite and grasping hands. But its ecosystem was ruled by rauisuchian crocodile-relatives larger than itself, so it occupied a mid-tier predatory role rather than the apex - the same kind of subordinate position dinosaurs held throughout the Triassic. Its world was a monsoonal floodplain of rivers and ferns, where it shared space with Eoraptor and the very first sauropodomorphs. Herrerasaurus sits near the origin of the meat-eating lineage , and its uncertain placement makes it a key test case for how Theropoda began. It shows large size was an early option for dinosaurs, decoupling 'getting big' from 'taking over'. Herrerasaurus corrects a tidy myth of progress: dinosaurs didn't start small and slowly build to giants and apex predators. Large hunters existed at the very beginning - they simply hadn't yet inherited a world still run by other reptiles.

title: Herrerasaurus — fossil record
text: Several partial skeletons including a well-preserved skull discovered in 1988 by Paul Sereno Evidence level: Partial. Ischigualasto Provincial Park, San Juan, Argentina Ischigualasto Formation Material: Skull, Partial skeleton, Limb bones, Vertebrae. Early Herrerasaurus material was fragmentary, but the 1988 discovery of a skull-bearing skeleton substantially improved our understanding of its anatomy. Even so, its position on the dinosaur family tree remains debated: it lacks some features shared by true theropods, yet its predatory toolkit is clearly theropod-like. The skull shows a mid-jaw hinge inferred to aid prey-gripping, though behavioural inferences from a single anatomical feature should be treated cautiously. Its temporal context - one of the oldest known large predatory dinosaurs - is well-established by the dated volcanic beds of the Ischigualasto.

title: Herrerasaurus — Is Herrerasaurus a true theropod or an early offshoot?
text: Is Herrerasaurus a true theropod or an early offshoot? It blends primitive saurischian traits with theropod-like predatory features. Early theropod (moderate confidence): Predatory specialisations align it with the meat-eating lineage. Basal saurischian outside Theropoda (moderate confidence): Primitive features suggest it branched before the theropod line proper.

title: Plateosaurus — overview
text: A large basal sauropodomorph from the Late Triassic of Central Europe, Plateosaurus was a bipedal-to-quadrupedal herbivore reaching around 8 metres and one of the first dinosaurs to achieve truly large body size. Known from hundreds of individuals across Germany, Switzerland, and Scandinavia, it also reveals that its growth pattern was fundamentally reptilian - slow and size-dependent - rather than the rapid, sustained growth seen in later dinosaurs. Gigantism, first draft: Long before the all-fours giants, Plateosaurus reached 8 metres on two legs - the sauropod project's prototype, proving 'large' was solved before 'colossal'. A biped that couldn't cheat: Its forelimbs couldn't rotate palm-down, so unlike Diplodocus or Brachiosaurus it was physically unable to walk on all fours and had to rear up to feed. Reptile-like growth: Same-aged adults vary wildly in size, pointing to food- and climate-driven growth more like a crocodile than the fast, fixed growth of birds and later dinosaurs. Mired by its own weight: Many skeletons sit in floodplain mud, best explained by heavy adults sinking where lighter juveniles escaped - a built-in size filter in the fossil record. Plateosaurus reared up to crop foliage other Triassic herbivores couldn't reach, swallowing stones to grind tough plants in its gut. By exploiting high browse , it sidestepped competition with the low-feeding reptiles around it - the same niche-splitting logic that would later let Brachiosaurus and Diplodocus share one landscape by feeding at different heights. It roamed seasonal European floodplains in apparent numbers, and the muddy mass graves suggest these were animals of open, low-lying ground. Plateosaurus previews the sauropod body plan - long neck, bulky body, fermenting gut - and shows that gigantism in this lineage began bipedally, with descendants only later dropping to all fours to carry even greater mass. Plateosaurus is the proof-of-concept for sauropod gigantism: before the 30-metre giants, evolution first had to solve merely being large - and this two-legged animal shows that solution in its earliest form.

title: Plateosaurus — fossil record
text: Dozens of partial to nearly complete skeletons from multiple European sites; one of the best-known early sauropodomorphs Evidence level: Rich. Trossingen and Halberstadt, Germany; Frick, Switzerland; other Central European sites Löwenstein Formation (Trossingen Beds); Knollenmergel Material: Multiple skeletons, Skull, Vertebrae, Limb bones. Plateosaurus is represented by over 100 individuals from many European localities, making it among the best-sampled early sauropodomorphs. This abundance has enabled bone histology studies revealing its distinctive developmental plasticity - individuals of the same skeletal age vary dramatically in body size, unlike the more clock-like growth of later dinosaurs. Mass-death sites where heavy individuals became mired in mud while lighter ones escaped suggest a taphonomic size filter that must be accounted for when interpreting the population data. The fossil record is good enough that the anatomy is well-constrained, though cranial material is less plentiful than postcranial.

title: Plateosaurus — Why do same-aged individuals differ so much in size?
text: Why do same-aged individuals differ so much in size? Growth rings in the bones don't track neatly with body size, which is unusual. Developmental plasticity (strong confidence): Growth flexed with food and climate, decoupling age from size - the favoured view. Multiple species (low confidence): Size classes might be different species, but most workers now reject this.

title: Dilophosaurus — overview
text: An early theropod from the Early Jurassic of Arizona, Dilophosaurus reached around 7 metres and is recognisable by two thin parallel crests running along the top of its skull, likely used for display or species recognition rather than combat. It was among the largest land predators of its time and place, and its actual anatomy - robust build, large teeth, capable forelimbs - is considerably more impressive than its cautious popular depictions. The film got it backwards: The neck frill and venom spit are pure invention - there is no fossil evidence for either; the real animal was simply a large, powerful apex predator. Crests too thin to fight with: Twin head crests were paper-thin and useless as weapons, marking them as display structures - an early instance of the visual signalling seen later in Parasaurolophus and pachycephalosaurs. Stronger than we thought: A 2020 redescription found it more robustly built than the weak-jawed scavenger of older reconstructions, with heavy neck and jaw muscle attachments. A crocodile's notch: A kink behind its front teeth echoes the snouts of fish-eating crocodiles , hinting at a waterside diet - a faint preview of the spinosaur strategy. Dilophosaurus hunted along Early Jurassic riverbanks, its powerful neck and jaws suited to delivering strong bites to fish and land prey alike. As the biggest predator in its environment, it held the apex role that Allosaurus would inherit later in the Jurassic, dominating ecosystems still recovering from the end-Triassic extinction. Its fragile, conspicuous crests are textbook signalling gear - used to recognise its own kind or compete for mates, not to wound. Dilophosaurus marks the first wave of large-bodied theropods , showing predators scaled up quickly once Triassic rivals vanished. Its display crests are an early chapter in the long dinosaur story of investing in ornament over armament. Dilophosaurus is a case study in popular culture overwriting science. Strip away the fictional frill and venom and you find something better: an early apex predator whose real anatomy records how big carnivores first re-evolved after a mass extinction.

title: Dilophosaurus — fossil record
text: Four partial skeletons from Arizona, with the best specimen including skull material; a 2020 redescription based on re-examination of all known material Evidence level: Partial. Navajo Nation, Arizona, USA Kayenta Formation Material: Skull, Partial skeleton, Vertebrae, Limb bones, Teeth. The four known Dilophosaurus specimens vary in completeness; the best-preserved skull has been re-studied in detail, leading to a 2020 monograph (Marsh & Rowe) that substantially revised our understanding of its musculature and build - producing a more robustly proportioned animal than earlier reconstructions. The delicate cranial crests are preserved but fragile, and their exact shape requires careful interpretation. No soft-tissue impressions are known, so the fictional frill and venom apparatus have no basis in the fossil record whatsoever. Footprints attributed to animals of this general size and period are known from the region but cannot be confidently assigned to this species.

title: Allosaurus — overview
text: An allosauroid theropod of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation, Allosaurus was the most abundant large predator of western North America, reaching around 8-9 metres with a deep skull, blade-like serrated teeth, and three-fingered hands bearing large curved claws. Known from dozens of specimens across a wide size range, it is one of the best-studied large Jurassic carnivores, and its skull biomechanics - a hatchet-strike impact rather than a sustained crushing bite - have been modelled in detail. A skull that swung like an axe: Engineering models show a skull weak in a steady clamp but strong against downward force - so it likely struck like a hatchet , a killing style no living animal uses, and the opposite of Tyrannosaurus's crushing bite. Jaws built to gape: Its lower jaw could swing exceptionally wide, reinforcing the slashing-strike model over a bone-crushing grip. A body covered in scars: Cleveland-Lloyd specimens carry abundant healed injuries , evidence of a violent life and a surprising ability to survive and recover from serious wounds. A predator trap: One quarry holds dozens of Allosaurus against few prey animals - a death trap that concentrated predators - one hypothesis likens it to a La Brea-style carnivore trap, though a drought-kill event has also been proposed. Allosaurus was the apex hunter of the Morrison Formation, sharing its world with Stegosaurus, Diplodocus and Apatosaurus. Biomechanics suggest it drove its upper jaw down into prey, then withdrew to let wounds work - a strike-and-retreat tactic suited to attacking animals far larger than itself, and the reason Stegosaurus tail spikes left healed punctures in Allosaurus bone. That predator-prey arms race, recorded directly in matching wounds, is some of the clearest evidence of Mesozoic combat we have. Allosaurus represents the allosauroids , the dominant large predators of the Jurassic before tyrannosaurs rose to fill the role in the north. Its specialised skull mechanics show theropods reaching very different solutions to the same problem of killing big prey. Allosaurus shows a predator's skull is a machine that can be reverse-engineered. By modelling its stresses, scientists reconstructed a hunting style with no modern equivalent - proof that deep time contains genuinely alien biology, not just bigger versions of today's animals.

title: Allosaurus — fossil record
text: Hundreds of bones from many individuals at the Cleveland-Lloyd Dinosaur Quarry, Utah; additional material from multiple Morrison Formation sites across western North America Evidence level: Exceptional. Cleveland-Lloyd Quarry, Utah; Dry Mesa, Colorado; many Morrison sites, USA Morrison Formation Material: Bonebed, Multiple skeletons, Skull, Teeth, Limb bones, Vertebrae. Allosaurus has one of the richest theropod fossil records anywhere. The Cleveland-Lloyd quarry alone has yielded over 10,000 bones representing at least 46 individuals - an extraordinary concentration that has made it a testbed for hypotheses about predator-trap dynamics, though what drew so many animals to one site is still debated. Healed bones showing injuries and pathologies are documented across the sample, providing direct evidence of a violent life and the animal's capacity for recovery. Finite-element analysis of the skull has been applied to constrain bite mechanics. This abundance means its anatomy and growth trajectory are well understood, though aspects of its behaviour are inferred from the bones rather than directly recorded.

title: Allosaurus — How did Allosaurus kill prey much larger than itself?
text: How did Allosaurus kill prey much larger than itself? Its skull is strong vertically but weak against twisting, ruling out a simple powerful clamp. Hatchet strike (strong confidence): Finite-element models support driving the upper jaw down as an axe-like blow. Pack hunting of sauropods (low confidence): Sometimes proposed, but direct evidence for cooperation is thin.

title: Stegosaurus — overview
text: A stegosaurian ornithischian of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation, Stegosaurus was a large slow-moving herbivore with a distinctive double row of upright bony plates along its back and a cluster of sharp spikes at the tail tip. The function of those plates - whether for thermoregulation, display, species recognition, or some combination - has been debated for decades without a settled answer, partly because no living animal offers a clear anatomical analogue. Armour that isn't armour: The back plates were thin and laced with blood vessels - badly placed to stop a bite, far better suited to display or shedding heat. The 'obvious' reading is the wrong one. A weapon with a kill record: The four tail spikes, nicknamed the 'thagomizer', left puncture wounds matching their size in Allosaurus bone - rare direct proof of which animal fought which. The walnut brain, overstated: Its brain was small but adequate; the famous 'second brain' over the hips was just a nerve enlargement , not a backup mind. A dedicated low-feeder: Its head hung near the ground, so it grazed ferns and cycads while the tall sauropods took the canopy - the height-based niche-splitting that defined Morrison plant-eaters. Stegosaurus processed large volumes of low, low-nutrient vegetation through a barrel-shaped gut, cropping with a weak beak. By feeding at ground level it avoided competing with Diplodocus and Brachiosaurus overhead, and when threatened by Allosaurus it could swing its spiked tail with bone-piercing force - the two species' fossils literally record their conflict. Whether the plates flushed with blood to signal mood or regulate temperature still colours how we picture the living animal. Stegosaurus is the flagship of the stegosaurs , an ornithischian experiment in vertical display and tail weaponry. It illustrates a recurring dinosaur theme: structures that look like armour usually turn out to be about communication instead. Stegosaurus teaches the core lesson of paleontology: the obvious interpretation is often wrong. The plates 'look like' armour and the spikes 'look like' decoration, yet careful study reverses both - display on the back, a lethal weapon at the tail.

title: Stegosaurus — fossil record
text: Several partial to nearly complete skeletons from Colorado and Wyoming; the best specimen (from Colorado) preserves plate arrangement Evidence level: Rich. Garden Park, Colorado; Como Bluff, Wyoming; Tendaguru, Tanzania Morrison Formation; Tendaguru Beds (Tanzania) Material: Partial skeleton, Skull, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Skin impressions. Multiple Stegosaurus specimens are known, and the overall body plan is well-established. The arrangement and internal structure of the dorsal plates - showing channels interpreted as blood-vessel pathways - are preserved in sufficient detail to fuel the ongoing thermoregulation vs. display debate, though the evidence is compatible with both. The tail spikes (thagomizer) have matched puncture marks in Allosaurus bone from the same formation, providing direct physical evidence of predator-prey interaction: a rare case of behaviour recorded in fossils. Stegosaurus material from Tanzania's Tendaguru Beds confirms a wide geographic range. The skull is relatively poorly represented compared to the postcranium.

title: Stegosaurus — What were the back plates actually for?
text: What were the back plates actually for? Their structure fits several functions, and they may have served more than one. Display / species recognition (strong confidence): Large, conspicuous and variable - strong signs of a visual signal. Thermoregulation (moderate confidence): Blood-vessel channels could shed or gather heat, though this is debated. Defense (low confidence): Too thin and poorly placed to be real armour; few now favour this.

title: Brachiosaurus — overview
text: A large macronarian sauropod of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation, Brachiosaurus was unusual among sauropods in having forelimbs longer than its hindlimbs, which tilted its body forward and directed its neck steeply upward in the manner of a giraffe. At an estimated 22 metres and up to 50 tonnes, it was one of the heaviest dinosaurs known from North America, and sustaining blood pressure to a head held some 9 metres above the ground would have required a powerful cardiovascular system. Giraffe posture at sauropod scale: Uniquely longer forelimbs tilted its body upward, lifting its head into treetops no other Morrison herbivore could reach - the high-browse counterpart to ground-feeding Diplodocus. A circulation problem: Raising blood several metres to a lifted head demands blood pressure beyond any living animal's , the physiological catch that fuels debate over how high it truly held its neck. Not the swamp-dweller of old books: High nostrils once suggested a snorkelling, water-bound life; it's now firmly a dry-land high-browser - an early misreading long since overturned. Breathing like a bird: Hollow vertebrae housed an air-sac lung system like a bird's , lightening the skeleton and helping oxygenate a vast body - evidence that avian breathing evolved deep in dinosaurs, long before flight. Brachiosaurus raked high conifer and cycad foliage with chisel-like teeth, swallowing it whole for a huge fermenting gut. Its entire niche was vertical reach : by feeding where almost nothing else could, it avoided competing with the low, wide-sweeping diplodocids that shared its floodplain. The avian air-sac system it carried - the same one that makes living birds efficient breathers - was part of what made such bulk physiologically possible; here, gigantism was as much about lungs and bone as about food. Brachiosaurus exemplifies the macronarian sauropods and the high-browsing answer to sauropod coexistence. Its skeleton is key evidence that the bird-style air-sac lung arose far down the dinosaur tree. Brachiosaurus forces biology and physics into one conversation. You can't understand it from bones alone - you have to ask whether a heart could even do what the skeleton demands, and that unresolved tension is exactly where the science lives.

title: Brachiosaurus — fossil record
text: Partial postcranial skeleton from Colorado (the type specimen); additional material including a well-known mounted skeleton in Berlin originally attributed to this genus but now assigned to the African species Giraffatitan Evidence level: Partial. Grand Junction, Colorado, USA; Tendaguru, Tanzania (Giraffatitan) Morrison Formation Material: Partial skeleton, Limb bones, Vertebrae. True North American Brachiosaurus material is less complete than is commonly assumed - the iconic Berlin mount and many popular illustrations are based largely on the African relative Giraffatitan brancai , which is far better represented. The type specimen of B. altithorax consists of dorsal vertebrae, coracoid, humerus, femur, and ribs - enough to establish its distinctive long-forelimbed proportions but not a complete picture. Cranial material of the true North American species is essentially unknown. The assignment of fragmentary material to this taxon is uncertain in many cases, so size and mass estimates carry wider error bars than popular accounts suggest.

title: Brachiosaurus — How high did Brachiosaurus actually hold its neck?
text: How high did Brachiosaurus actually hold its neck? A fully raised neck is costly to supply with blood, pitting skeleton against physiology. Habitually high (moderate confidence): Limb proportions and high-browser teeth suggest genuine canopy feeding. More horizontal at rest (moderate confidence): Blood-pressure costs imply it may have raised the neck only to feed.

title: Diplodocus — overview
text: A diplodocid sauropod of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation, Diplodocus was one of the longest land animals known, stretching around 26 metres with a slender build, an extremely long whip-like tail, and a small head equipped only with pencil-like peg teeth for raking and stripping vegetation rather than chewing it. It is one of the best-known sauropods, thanks in part to Barnum Brown's 1899 excavation; casts of the Carnegie Museum skeleton were distributed to major museums worldwide in the early 20th century. A rake, not a mouth: Slender pencil teeth bunched at the front of the jaw suggest it stripped leaves off branches by drawing them through - a sharp contrast to Brachiosaurus's chisel-bite plucking. A tail that may have cracked: Its whip-thin tail has been modelled as snapping faster than sound , potentially as a noise-maker or deterrent rather than a club. Built to stand still and reach: Likely holding its long neck near-horizontal, it could sweep a wide arc of low vegetation without moving its bulk - feeding by reach, not by walking, to save energy. Teeth on a fortnightly schedule: It replaced each tooth every few weeks, an extreme turnover betraying a diet of gritty, abrasive plants that wore teeth out fast. Diplodocus harvested huge volumes of low-to-mid vegetation from a near-stationary stance, an energy-saving tactic for so massive an animal, while its rapid tooth replacement tracked a tough, abrasive diet. Sharing the Morrison floodplain with high-feeding Brachiosaurus and ground-grazing Stegosaurus, it occupied the wide-sweep low-browser slot - three giants partitioning one landscape by how and where they fed. The whip-tail may have served to signal, deter rivals, or defend against Allosaurus, even if it never truly broke the sound barrier. Diplodocus typifies the diplodocids and their low-browsing strategy, the mirror image of Brachiosaurus's high reach. Together they show how sauropods divided the plant world by neck posture , letting multiple colossi coexist. Diplodocus shows that size is a strategy, not just spectacle. Long neck, raking teeth and fast-replacing dentition are all organised around one principle - move the least while eating the most - a solution evolution reached at colossal scale.

title: Diplodocus — fossil record
text: Multiple partial to complete skeletons; the Carnegie Museum specimen (CM 84) is one of the most reproduced sauropod skeletons in history, cast for museums worldwide Evidence level: Rich. Wyoming, Colorado, Utah, USA Morrison Formation Material: Multiple skeletons, Skull, Vertebrae, Limb bones, Teeth. Diplodocus is among the best-known sauropods. CM 84 was excavated in 1899 and formally described by Hatcher in 1901; the original skeleton was mounted at the Carnegie Museum in 1907, and casts were gifted to museums across the world - making it the most widely distributed sauropod skeleton of the early 20th century. Multiple specimens allow assessment of individual variation and growth. Pencil-like teeth replaced rapidly - confirmed by tooth-wear and replacement patterns in the fossil material - directly supporting a high-volume, abrasive-plant diet. The whip-tail hypothesis is based on the extreme thinning of tail vertebrae; the actual soft-tissue tip that would confirm a sonic crack is never preserved, making that claim inferential.

title: Diplodocus — Could the tail really crack like a whip?
text: Could the tail really crack like a whip? Models suggest it's possible, but the soft tail-tip that would matter is never preserved. Supersonic whip (low confidence): Simulations allow it, but the fragile tip needed to confirm it is always lost. Signalling / defence (moderate confidence): The tail was likely used to deter or communicate even without a sonic 'crack'.

title: Archaeopteryx — overview
text: A crow-sized avialan theropod from the Late Jurassic lagoons of Bavaria, Archaeopteryx combined asymmetric flight feathers with a suite of ancestral features - toothed jaws, clawed fingers, and a long bony tail - that its non-flying dinosaurian relatives still carried. Known from twelve or thirteen specimens in the Solnhofen limestone, some preserving individual feather vanes and partial colour evidence from melanosomes, it sits at the boundary between non-avian dinosaurs and birds; the precise mode of its flight - how capable, and whether originating from the trees or the ground - remains actively debated. Half-reptile, half-bird, literally: It has teeth, a bony tail and clawed fingers like its dromaeosaur cousins, but flight feathers laid out exactly as in a living pigeon - the intermediate made flesh. Preserved feather by feather: The ultra-fine Solnhofen limestone captured individual feather impressions , which is why its 1861 discovery, two years after Darwin, landed like a thunderclap. An awkward flier: Asymmetric feathers prove some aerodynamic function, but a weak shoulder and small breastbone mean it lacked the muscle anchor for strong, sustained flapping. A 150-million-year-old colour: Fossilised pigment structures reveal at least partly black plumage - recovering an actual colour from the Jurassic, not just a shape. Archaeopteryx was likely a glider and burst-flier rather than a sustained flapper, using slopes, trees or running starts to get airborne, and hunting insects and small animals with clawed hands and toothed jaws. It lived on a subtropical archipelago fringing a warm sea, moving between ground and low vegetation - a small predator filling the agile-insectivore role alongside the similarly sized Compsognathus on those same islands. Its delicate build paints an animal of edges and undergrowth, not the open sky. Archaeopteryx is the keystone evidence that birds are dinosaurs . It handed Victorian science a literal intermediate and still anchors debates over whether powered flight began in the trees or on the ground. Archaeopteryx made evolution visible. Arriving just after Darwin's Origin, it supplied the exact 'missing link' critics demanded - and 160 years on it still does real scientific work, anchoring the link between dinosaurs and the birds outside the window.

title: Archaeopteryx — fossil record
text: Twelve or thirteen specimens from the Solnhofen Limestone including feather impressions; the London specimen (1861) and Berlin specimen (1877) are the most complete Evidence level: Exceptional. Solnhofen, Bavaria, Germany Solnhofen Limestone (Plattenkalk) Material: Nearly complete skeleton, Skull, Feathers, Teeth, Limb bones, Claw. The Solnhofen Limestone is one of the finest Lagerstätten in the world, preserving soft tissues - including individual feather vanes - with extraordinary fidelity in lithographic-quality limestone. This means Archaeopteryx is known not just from bone but from asymmetric flight feathers whose aerodynamic implications can be studied directly. Preserved melanosomes in at least the London specimen have allowed researchers to infer partial black colouration. The 1861 discovery - two years after Darwin's Origin - meant its transitional anatomy had immediate and lasting impact. Despite this exceptional record, the skeleton's functional interpretation remains debated: shoulder geometry and breastbone size are consistent with limited or awkward flight, though the exact flight capability is still argued. The bone itself shows a mix of reptile and bird features visible in the original material, not inferred from reconstruction.

title: Archaeopteryx — How did flight begin - from the trees down or the ground up?
text: How did flight begin - from the trees down or the ground up? It had wings but lacked a modern bird's full flight apparatus, leaving its capability ambiguous. Arboreal (trees-down) (moderate confidence): Gliding from heights could have preceded flapping flight. Cursorial (ground-up) (moderate confidence): Running and leaping with proto-wings may have led to flight. Wing-assisted incline running (moderate confidence): Flapping to scramble up slopes, as living birds do, offers a middle path.

title: Compsognathus — overview
text: A small compsognathid theropod from the Late Jurassic Solnhofen lagoons of Bavaria, Compsognathus was roughly the size of a chicken and one of the smallest non-avian dinosaurs known at the time of its description. One specimen preserves a swallowed lizard in its gut region, providing a direct record of its diet, and its close resemblance to Archaeopteryx from the same formation was historically important in linking small carnivorous theropods to the bird lineage. Its last meal, fossilised: A skeleton preserves a swallowed lizard whole in its gut - direct, undeniable evidence of diet, the kind of detail a thousand ordinary bones never give. Speed in miniature: Long legs and a feather-light frame mark it as a fast, darting hunter , the small-game specialist of its island world. A 19th-century bird clue: Its close resemblance to Archaeopteryx helped early scientists argue that birds descend from small theropods - two animals from the same lagoon bracketing the bird transition. A dinosaur the size of a hen: At 2-3 kg it punctures the giants-only image of dinosaurs; most Mesozoic ecosystems, like modern ones, ran mostly on small animals . Compsognathus chased lizards and insects across the dry islands ringing the Solnhofen lagoon, its preserved gut lizard showing it took relatively large, swift prey for its size. In that island ecosystem it was the small-predator counterpart to Archaeopteryx, the two dividing small prey while leaving larger game to bigger hunters elsewhere. Its presence is a reminder that the famous giants were the minority - the base of every food web is small and fast. Compsognathus was historically vital for connecting birds to small theropods , its skeleton resembling Archaeopteryx minus the feathers. It anchors the small-bodied end of the predatory dinosaur spectrum. Compsognathus corrects a deep bias: dinosaurs are remembered as enormous, yet most ecosystems were built on small animals. And its intact last meal shows how a single exceptional fossil can reveal what countless ordinary ones cannot.

title: Compsognathus — fossil record
text: Two specimens: a juvenile or sub-adult from Bavaria (Germany) and an adult from Canjuers, France; the German specimen preserves gut contents Evidence level: Partial. Solnhofen, Bavaria, Germany; Canjuers, Var, France Solnhofen Limestone (Germany); Portlandian limestones (France) Material: Partial skeleton, Skull, Teeth, Limb bones. Only two Compsognathus specimens are known, limiting what can be said about individual variation or population biology. The German specimen is famously associated with the remains of a small lizard ( Bavariasaurus ) in its gut region, providing direct dietary evidence - one of the clearest examples of preserved last-meal contents in the fossil record. Size differences between the two specimens once prompted multiple-species hypotheses; these are now generally interpreted as growth stage differences rather than separate species. Its small size in the Solnhofen fauna places it as one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs, though the juvenile German specimen may underrepresent adult dimensions.

title: Yi qi — overview
text: A pigeon-sized scansoriopterygid theropod from the Late Jurassic of Hebei, China, Yi qi is remarkable for an elongated rod-like wrist bone that appears to have supported a bat-like wing membrane rather than the feathered wings seen in other paravian dinosaurs. Known from a single specimen, it represents a unique experiment in dinosaurian aerial locomotion that has no known descendants. Bat wings on a dinosaur: Instead of a feathered airfoil, it stretched skin membranes supported by a bizarre extra wrist rod - flight gear more like a bat or flying squirrel than a bird. A failed alternative timeline: While Archaeopteryx's feathered design led to all living birds, Yi qi's membrane route was an evolutionary dead end - a glimpse of how flight could have gone, but didn't. A bone no one expected: That long styliform wrist bone has no parallel in other dinosaurs, making the animal hard to reconstruct and its exact wing shape genuinely uncertain . Probably a glider, not a flapper: Its anatomy suggests gliding between trees rather than powered flight, like a modern flying squirrel rather than a sparrow. Yi qi likely clambered through Jurassic forests and glided between trees on membrane wings, feeding on insects and small fare. It occupied an arboreal glider niche that no surviving lineage inherited, exploiting the canopy in parallel with the feathered proto-birds experimenting nearby - two rival blueprints for getting airborne, tested at the same time. Because the membrane and its support are so unusual, even how it held its wings remains debated. Yi qi shows that the origin of flight involved more than one design , not a single inevitable march toward birds. Its skin wings are a reminder that evolution explores blind alleys - and that the bird solution was one option among several, not a foregone conclusion. Yi qi reframes the origin of flight as a series of experiments rather than a straight line. Birds feel inevitable only in hindsight; this membrane-winged oddity shows the path was contingent, branching, and full of designs that simply didn't last.

title: Yi qi — fossil record
text: A single specimen preserving the unusual styliform wrist element and feather impressions Evidence level: Partial. Qinglong County, Hebei, China Tiaojishan Formation Material: Partial skeleton, Feathers, Limb bones, Skull. Yi qi is known from one specimen (STM 31-2), described in 2015. The preserved styliform wrist bone - the rod-like extension that would have supported a wing membrane - has no parallel in other theropods and cannot be fully interpreted by analogy with living animals. Whether the membrane was more bat-like or flying-squirrel-like, and how it was held during flight or gliding, must be inferred from the bone geometry alone. Because this is a single specimen, individual variation cannot be assessed and some unusual features might reflect individual pathology. The Tiaojishan Formation has produced excellent preservation, but this specimen alone cannot resolve exactly how Yi qi moved through the air.

title: Yi qi — Could Yi qi truly fly, or only glide?
text: Could Yi qi truly fly, or only glide? Its membrane wing is unlike anything living, so its aerodynamic limits are hard to model. Gliding only (moderate confidence): Limited flight muscle suggests passive gliding between trees. Limited flapping flight (low confidence): Some argue the membranes allowed brief powered flight, but evidence is thin.

title: Spinosaurus — overview
text: A spinosaurid theropod from Late Cretaceous North Africa, Spinosaurus reached an estimated 15 metres in length - among the longest known predatory dinosaurs - and is recognised by a long, narrow fish-catching skull, cone-shaped unserrated teeth, and a tall dorsal sail of elongated bony spines. Its bone density and oxygen isotope chemistry both point to a life more closely tied to water than any other large theropod, but whether it was an active swimmer or hunted from the shallows remains one of the most actively contested questions in the field, complicated by the wartime loss of the original type specimen. A sail no one can fully explain: A tall sail of bony spines ran down its back, argued to be for display, heat exchange, or fat storage - its function remains one of the genuinely open questions in the field. A crocodile's mouth on a giant: Cone-shaped, unserrated teeth and a long narrow snout are built to grip slippery fish , not slice flesh - convergent with the same design seen in its smaller relative Baryonyx. Diving bones: Unusually dense, solid bones echo those of diving animals like penguins and hippos, a leading argument for a genuinely water-based life. The skeleton lost to war: The first good specimen was destroyed in a 1944 bombing raid , so today's fierce debate rests on newer, frustratingly incomplete finds. Spinosaurus clearly centred its life on water - teeth, snout, and oxygen isotope ratios in its bones - a signature of time spent in water - all point to a life centred on aquatic environments and fish prey. Whether it pursued prey by swimming or stalked it like a colossal heron is the live question. Either way, it was tied to rivers in a way no other huge theropod was, hunting the giant fish and crocodile relatives of a vast Saharan river system and sidestepping competition with land-based predators by exploiting an aquatic larder they couldn't reach. That riverine specialisation is the most extreme ecological departure in the whole theropod story. Spinosaurus shows theropods invading the water , the sharpest break from the standard land-predator body plan in the group. It demonstrates how far a lineage can be pushed from its ancestral way of life. Spinosaurus is paleontology unfolding in real time. With a lost original skeleton and dramatic new finds, specialists openly disagree in print - vivid proof that the science is not settled history but an active, contested investigation.

title: Spinosaurus — fossil record
text: Original North African skeleton (Bahariya, Egypt) described by Stromer in 1915 and destroyed in a 1944 Allied bombing raid; partial new specimens from the Kem Kem region of Morocco recovered since 2008, including vertebrae, a partial skull, and most significantly a 2020 specimen providing tail material Evidence level: Fragments. Bahariya Oasis, Egypt (lost); Kem Kem Beds, southeastern Morocco Bahariya Formation (lost material); Kem Kem Group Material: Vertebrae, Skull, Teeth, Limb bones. The fate of the original type specimen - lost to war - is genuinely central to understanding what we do and do not know about Spinosaurus . Stromer's original descriptions and drawings are the only record of that material. New specimens from the Kem Kem are real and important but fragmentary; the 2014 reconstruction by Ibrahim et al. that proposed short hindlimbs and aquatic locomotion was based on partial material and a composite reconstruction that attracted significant peer criticism regarding how individual bones were assigned to one animal. The 2020 tail specimen showed paddle-like vertebral proportions supporting some aquatic use. But the overall body proportions, exact size, and locomotory mode of this animal remain among the most contested issues in current palaeontology - an unusually honest reflection of how limited the physical evidence actually is.

title: Spinosaurus — Was Spinosaurus an active swimmer or a shoreline wader?
text: Was Spinosaurus an active swimmer or a shoreline wader? Bone density and tail shape suggest swimming, but limb proportions and balance complicate it - and key fossils were lost in 1944. Pursuit swimmer (moderate confidence): Dense bones and a paddle-like tail suggest it chased fish underwater. Wading ambusher (moderate confidence): Biomechanics hint it was an unstable swimmer that hunted from the shallows. Riverbank generalist (moderate confidence): It may have done both, feeding in and beside the water as chance allowed.

title: Tyrannosaurus rex — overview
text: A massive tyrannosaurid theropod of the latest Cretaceous, Tyrannosaurus rex was the apex predator of western North America, built around an enormous skull with thick bone-crushing teeth, binocular vision, and acute olfaction. More than fifty known specimens make it one of the best-quantified Mesozoic animals: its bite force, growth rate, and sensory capabilities have all been measured directly, giving the most iconic predatory dinosaur an unusually rigorous scientific foundation. The strongest bite of any land animal: Modelled at several tonnes of force, its bite could crush bone outright - the opposite strategy to Allosaurus's slashing axe-strike, and one few animals before or since have matched. Teeth like railroad spikes: Thick, banana-shaped teeth weren't for slicing but for shattering carcasses , letting it extract nutrients from bone that other predators left behind. A teenage growth spurt: Bone rings show it gained over 600 kg a year through its teens - explosive, bird-and-mammal-like growth utterly unlike the slow reptilian pattern of Plateosaurus. Senses tuned for hunting: A huge olfactory region and forward-facing, binocular eyes gave it keen smell and depth perception - the toolkit of an active hunter, not merely a scavenger. Tyrannosaurus was the apex predator of its world, almost certainly both hunting live prey and scavenging - its bone-crushing bite let it exploit carcasses no rival could fully use, a competitive edge as much as a weapon. It preyed on the herbivores sharing its landscape, Triceratops and the duck-billed hadrosaurs, and frill injuries on Triceratops hint at encounters that didn't always go the predator's way . Possible juvenile and adult feeding differences suggest a single species may have split the food supply by age , young rexes taking smaller, faster prey than the bone-crushing adults. Tyrannosaurus caps the tyrannosaurids , which evolved giant size in the north independently of the southern carcharodontosaurs like Giganotosaurus - two lineages converging on the apex role with completely different jaws. It is the benchmark against which other giant predators are measured. Tyrannosaurus shows that fame and rigour can coincide. Behind the icon is one of the best-quantified animals in all of paleontology - its bite force, growth rate and senses measured, not imagined - proving that even the most mythologised fossil can be pinned down by evidence.

title: Tyrannosaurus rex — fossil record
text: Over 50 specimens of varying completeness including several nearly complete skeletons; 'Sue' (FMNH PR2081) at Chicago's Field Museum is the largest and most complete known individual Evidence level: Exceptional. Montana, South Dakota, Wyoming, Colorado, USA; Saskatchewan, Canada Hell Creek Formation; Lance Formation; Scollard Formation Material: Nearly complete skeleton, Skull, Teeth, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Bonebed. Tyrannosaurus rex is one of the best-represented large theropods in the fossil record. The sample is large enough to study growth trajectories through bone rings, document pathologies and healed injuries, examine sexual dimorphism hypotheses, and - controversially - identify possible soft-tissue preservation in medullary bone (the 'B-rex' specimen, MOR 1125). Bite marks attributed to T. rex are preserved on hadrosaur and ceratopsian bones, providing physical evidence of feeding and even predation attempts (healed bites show the prey survived). The size range across specimens has fuelled debate about whether robust and gracile morphs represent sex differences or separate species. For all this material, behaviour beyond feeding is inferred, not directly recorded.

title: Tyrannosaurus rex — Was T. rex primarily a hunter or a scavenger?
text: Was T. rex primarily a hunter or a scavenger? It has features cited for both, and the truth is likely a mix that's hard to weight precisely. Active hunter (strong confidence): Binocular vision, speed and healed prey injuries point to genuine predation. Opportunistic scavenger too (strong confidence): Its bone-crushing bite and acute smell suit exploiting carcasses as well.

title: Giganotosaurus — overview
text: A large carcharodontosaurid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia, Giganotosaurus was one of the longest known predatory dinosaurs at an estimated 13 metres, sharing its world with the giant titanosaur sauropods that were likely its main prey. Unlike Tyrannosaurus, it carried narrow blade-like teeth suited to slashing and wounding rather than shattering bone - a different approach to large-prey predation, independently evolved on a different continent. Same size, opposite weapon: It matched Tyrannosaurus in length but carried thin, blade-like slicing teeth instead of bone-crushers - two giants, two entirely different killing systems, on two continents. A hint of pack life: Its close relative Mapusaurus is known from many individuals together , raising the real possibility these slashers hunted cooperatively against giant prey. Big but lightly built: Longer and taller than T. rex yet more gracile , it traded crushing power for reach and slashing speed. Sized to wound titans: It shared its world with colossal long-necks, and its slicing bite suits opening deep wounds in huge prey rather than killing in a single blow. Giganotosaurus likely attacked large sauropods with slashing bites that bled prey out, and the herd evidence from Mapusaurus suggests its family may have hunted in groups to bring down animals many times their mass - a strategy that could overcome titanosaurs no solitary predator could tackle. It ruled Patagonia tens of millions of years before T. rex appeared in the north, so the two were never rivals. Instead they are parallel experiments : the same ecological job filled by separate lineages reaching the same scale. Giganotosaurus is a flagship carcharodontosaurid , the slashing giant predators that ruled the southern continents. Its existence proves tyrannosaur-scale carnivory evolved more than once, independently . Giganotosaurus breaks the assumption that T. rex was the template for all giant predators. A different lineage, on a different continent, reached the same size with completely different jaws - convergence on giants, divergence in how they killed.

title: Giganotosaurus — fossil record
text: A single partial skeleton (MUCPv-Ch1) representing roughly 70% of the skeleton, described in 1995; a second fragmentary maxilla (jaw fragment) assigned to this genus Evidence level: Partial. Neuquén Province, Patagonia, Argentina Candeleros Formation (Río Limay Subgroup) Material: Partial skeleton, Skull, Teeth, Limb bones, Vertebrae. The holotype of Giganotosaurus is substantial enough to confirm the general body plan and establish it as one of the largest known theropods, but it is a single individual and some elements are incomplete. Size comparisons with T. rex depend on scaling from partial material, so precise length and mass figures carry real uncertainty - claims of it being 'larger than T. rex' should be read with that caveat. The closely related Mapusaurus roseae is known from a multi-individual bonebed, and evidence of gregarious behaviour is sometimes attributed by extension to Giganotosaurus ; this is an inference, not directly evidenced for this taxon.

title: Giganotosaurus — Did Giganotosaurus and its relatives hunt in groups?
text: Did Giganotosaurus and its relatives hunt in groups? Group evidence comes mainly from a related species, so applying it here is an inference. Cooperative pack hunters (moderate confidence): The multi-individual Mapusaurus site hints at social hunting in the family. Solitary giants (moderate confidence): Aggregations can form without cooperation; solitary hunting can't be ruled out.

title: Carnotaurus — overview
text: An abelisaurid theropod from Late Cretaceous Patagonia, Carnotaurus was a medium-sized predator with two stout brow horns, forelimbs reduced nearly to vestigial stubs, and a build that points to unusual speed for a carnivore its size. Known from a single but exceptionally complete skeleton - one of the few large theropods with extensive fossilised skin impressions - it is the best-documented abelisaurid and the most familiar representative of the large carnivores that dominated Gondwana's southern continents through the Cretaceous. Arms even smaller than T. rex's: Its forelimbs were so reduced as to be near-useless , with the bones almost fused - taking the tiny-arm trend of big theropods to its absurd extreme. Built to run: Enormous tail muscles, revealed by its vertebrae, powered a fast-running build unusual for so large a predator - speed as its primary weapon. The only horned big predator: Paired brow horns, unique among large carnivores, were likely for head-to-head shoving between rivals rather than killing prey. We know its skin: Fossilised skin impressions show a hide of non-overlapping scales with larger studs - rare direct evidence of a predatory dinosaur's actual texture. Carnotaurus was a lightweight, fast pursuit predator that likely ran down agile prey and delivered quick slashing bites, its weak neck and small teeth suiting rapid strikes over prolonged grappling. As the dominant abelisaurid of southern South America, it filled the apex role that tyrannosaurs held in the north - another case of different lineages, same job , echoing Giganotosaurus before it. The brow horns most plausibly settled contests between Carnotaurus rather than serving against prey, the southern equivalent of the head-butting and frill-jousting seen in herbivores. Carnotaurus is the most completely known abelisaurid , the predators that dominated Gondwana after it split from the northern continents. Its bizarre proportions show how isolation breeds specialisation - speed and horns up front, arms all but discarded. Carnotaurus shows how geographic isolation drives evolution down strange roads. Cut off on a southern landmass, the abelisaurids built an apex predator around speed and horns while letting the arms wither - a body no northern lineage ever produced.

title: Carnotaurus — fossil record
text: A single specimen (MACN-CH 894) that is unusually complete for a large theropod, including well-preserved skin impressions Evidence level: Rich. Chubut Province, Patagonia, Argentina La Colonia Formation Material: Nearly complete skeleton, Skull, Skin impressions, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Teeth. The single known Carnotaurus skeleton is remarkable for its exceptional skin preservation: extensive patches of non-overlapping scales with larger bony studs are preserved across much of the body, giving us direct evidence of the hide texture that most dinosaur reconstructions can only guess at. This places it among a small group of large theropods for which we have skin evidence. The brow horns are preserved in place, confirming their general position and relative size. Being a single individual limits what can be said about growth or variation. The forelimbs are so reduced that the preserved elements show near-fusion of bones, directly confirming the functional redundancy of the arms that postcranial anatomy implies.

title: Carnotaurus — What were the tiny arms and the horns actually for?
text: What were the tiny arms and the horns actually for? The arms are too reduced for obvious use, and the horns are unique among big predators. Arms: vestigial (strong confidence): So reduced they likely had little function - a lineage shedding unused structure. Horns: rival combat / display (moderate confidence): Best explained as tools for shoving matches or signalling between Carnotaurus.

title: Baryonyx — overview
text: A spinosaurid theropod from the Early Cretaceous of England, Baryonyx was around 9 metres long with a long, narrow, crocodile-like skull, conical teeth, and a large curved thumb claw. Its preserved stomach contents include fish scales and bones of a young Iguanodon, providing direct physical evidence of a mixed diet that placed the spinosaurid fish-eating hypothesis on solid empirical ground. The smoking gun for fish-eating: Fish scales and a young Iguanodon's bones preserved in its gut directly proved a mixed diet - the physical evidence that grounds the whole Spinosaurus debate. A thumb claw like a gaff: A huge curved hand claw likely worked as a fishing hook , flicking prey from the water the way a grizzly bear hooks salmon. A crocodile's snout, again: Its long narrow jaw with conical teeth mirrors a fish-eating crocodile's - the same convergent design that reappears, scaled up, in its relative Spinosaurus. Found by an amateur: An amateur collector spotted the giant claw in a clay pit in 1983, a reminder that major discoveries still come from outside academia . Baryonyx hunted along Early Cretaceous rivers, snatching fish with its narrow jaws and hooked claw while also taking land prey - the swallowed Iguanodon shows it was no strict fish-eater but a riverbank generalist . By working the water's edge it tapped a food supply the purely terrestrial predators of its lush wetland largely ignored, easing competition. Its discovery reframed spinosaurids from baffling oddities into comprehensible semi-aquatic hunters. Baryonyx is the spinosaurid whose preserved stomach contents confirmed the group's fish-eating habits, anchoring the larger Spinosaurus story in hard evidence and documenting a major theropod move toward aquatic food . Baryonyx proves how a single well-preserved gut can settle decades of speculation. Before it, fish-eating in big theropods was a guess; after it, the spinosaurid story stood on physical evidence rather than imagination.

title: Baryonyx — fossil record
text: A single partial skeleton from the Smokejacks Pit (Weald Clay Formation), Surrey, England, including a large thumb claw; fish scales and juvenile Iguanodon bones preserved in the gut region Evidence level: Partial. Smokejacks Pit, Ockley, Surrey, England Wealden Supergroup (Weald Clay Formation, Surrey) Material: Partial skeleton, Skull, Claw, Teeth, Limb bones. The holotype skeleton of Baryonyx walkeri , discovered by amateur collector William Walker in 1983, is the primary basis for everything we know about this animal. The gut contents - fish scales of Lepidotes and bones of a juvenile Iguanodon - are among the most direct dietary evidence in any theropod, establishing a mixed piscivorous and terrestrial diet beyond reasonable doubt. Additional spinosaurid material from the Iberian Peninsula and Portugal has been referred to closely related taxa, extending the family's range. The skull, while partial, shows the narrow crocodile-like snout clearly. Gaps in the skeleton mean that overall size estimates are extrapolated from the known material.

title: Iguanodon — overview
text: A large iguanodontian ornithopod of the Early Cretaceous, Iguanodon was around 10 metres long and able to move on two or four legs, processing tough plant material with a well-developed beak and batteries of grinding teeth, plus a distinctive conical thumb spike. One of the first dinosaurs formally described, in 1825, its reconstruction changed dramatically over 150 years - from a four-legged iguana-like monster with a nose horn to a bipedal kangaroo posture to the more accurate modern depiction - tracing the history of how palaeontology built its picture of extinct animals. A thumb mistaken for a horn: Its famous thumb spike was first stuck on the snout as a nose horn - a vivid lesson, paralleling Triceratops, in how easily isolated bones are misread. A Swiss-army hand: Its hand combined a defensive thumb spike, weight-bearing middle fingers and a grasping little finger - a single limb doing feeding, walking and defence . Two-legged or four, on demand: Trackways and limb proportions show it could switch posture , walking on four legs to travel and rearing on two to feed or flee. A herd in the coal: Dozens of skeletons from a Belgian mine suggest these animals moved and died in groups , unusual evidence of social life in an early ornithopod. Iguanodon was a bulk plant-processor whose skull flexed as it chewed, grinding tough vegetation with packed tooth batteries - an advanced system that foreshadowed the runaway success of the hadrosaurs like Parasaurolophus. Browsing low and mid-height plants and dropping to all fours to cover ground, it was the dominant mid-sized herbivore of its European wetlands, with its thumb spike a ready defence against predators such as Baryonyx. The Bernissart mass grave points to genuinely gregarious behaviour, not just chance accumulation. Iguanodon sits just below the hadrosaurs , the most successful Late Cretaceous plant-eaters, and shows their key chewing innovations taking shape. Named in 1825, it also helped define the very concept of a dinosaur. Iguanodon is the history of paleontology in one animal. From a misplaced nose-horn to a sophisticated reconstruction, its story shows our picture of the past is a self-correcting method that improves - not a fixed truth handed down.

title: Iguanodon — fossil record
text: Dozens of partial to complete skeletons from the Bernissart coal mine, Belgium (1878), plus fragmentary material from across Europe; one of the earliest and most extensively studied dinosaurs Evidence level: Exceptional. Bernissart, Belgium; Wealden sites in England, Spain, Germany Wealden Supergroup; Sainte-Barbe Clays (Bernissart) Material: Multiple skeletons, Skull, Limb bones, Teeth, Vertebrae, Tracks. The 1878 Bernissart discovery - 38 articulated skeletons extracted from a Belgian coal mine over four years - transformed palaeontology's ability to study a single dinosaur species. This material enabled direct comparison between individuals, study of growth, and the mounting of multiple complete skeletons. The famous misplaced thumb spike was identified as such once complete hand material from Bernissart made the anatomy clear - an early lesson in how isolated specimens mislead. Iguanodon tracks are known from several European sites. Being among the earliest named dinosaurs (1825), the history of its reconstruction charts the whole arc of changing understanding, from Mantell's initial fragments to the Bernissart bounty.

title: Deinonychus — overview
text: A medium-sized dromaeosaurid theropod from the Early Cretaceous of the western USA, Deinonychus was around 3.4 metres long and built for active predation, with a large sickle-shaped claw on each second toe that was held raised off the ground and used to pin or slash prey. John Ostrom's 1969 description was pivotal in establishing that at least some dinosaurs were active and agile rather than slow and cold-blooded, helping to reorient the whole science. The claw that overturned a stereotype: Its enlarged sickle claw implied an agile, energetic hunter , single-handedly toppling the 1960s image of dinosaurs as sluggish, cold-blooded failures. The discovery that revived birds: Its bird-like skeleton resurrected the idea that birds are living dinosaurs - once fringe, now overwhelmingly accepted, and visible in cousins like Velociraptor. A grip, not a slash: Long imagined as a slashing blade, the claw is now modelled as a gripping spike that pinned prey while the animal fed - the way a hawk holds a struggling rodent. A clue to teamwork: Several Deinonychus found with one large plant-eater hint at group attacks , though it may equally record a chaotic feeding frenzy. Deinonychus was a fast, athletic predator that likely used its sickle claws to pin prey while gripping with its hands and feeding, balancing through quick turns with a stiffened tail - the 'raptor prey restraint' model drawn directly from how modern birds of prey subdue victims. In its ecosystem it was the mid-sized active hunter , taking prey too large for small theropods but beneath the notice of giants. Whether it truly coordinated in packs or simply mobbed kills is debated, but its energy and bird-like biology are not. Deinonychus launched the Dinosaur Renaissance , reframing dinosaurs as dynamic and warm-blooded and cementing the dinosaur-bird link . Few single discoveries have so completely rewired a field's worldview. Deinonychus is the clearest case of one fossil rewriting a science. Its claw didn't just name a new animal - it overturned the belief that dinosaurs were evolutionary dead ends and pointed straight at the birds alive today.

title: Deinonychus — fossil record
text: Several partial skeletons from Montana, including material from a site associated with the herbivore Tenontosaurus; John Ostrom's 1969 description revolutionised theropod palaeontology Evidence level: Partial. Big Horn County, Montana; Carbon County, Montana, USA Cloverly Formation Material: Partial skeleton, Skull, Claw, Limb bones, Teeth, Vertebrae. Multiple Deinonychus specimens are known but none is complete; the skull in particular is known from fragments across several individuals rather than one intact cranium. The sickle claw is well-preserved and its curvature and joint mechanics have been modelled to test the slashing vs. gripping hypotheses - the physical evidence favours gripping over slashing, matching prey-restraint models from living raptorial birds. The association of Deinonychus bones with Tenontosaurus at some sites has been interpreted as evidence for group hunting, but a single site taphonomy can equally reflect opportunistic scavenging or individual deaths at a food source over time. No direct evidence of cooperative behaviour exists in the fossils.

title: Deinonychus — Did Deinonychus hunt in coordinated packs?
text: Did Deinonychus hunt in coordinated packs? Sites with several individuals and one prey animal are suggestive but can form by scavenging. Coordinated pack (low confidence): The classic interpretation, but true cooperation is hard to prove from bones. Loose mobbing (moderate confidence): Like modern reptiles, they may have gathered at a kill without real teamwork.

title: Microraptor — overview
text: A crow-sized dromaeosaurid theropod from the Early Cretaceous of Liaoning, China, Microraptor bore long flight feathers on both its arms and hindlimbs - a four-winged configuration with no living parallel - and is widely interpreted as an arboreal glider or short-range flier. Preserved gut contents across different specimens record birds, mammals, and fish; melanosome analysis has identified an iridescent black plumage comparable to a modern starling, making it one of the few Mesozoic animals for which both diet and colour are directly evidenced. Wings on its legs too: It bore long flight feathers on its hindlimbs as well as its arms , a 'four-winged' layout with no living parallel - flight being tried in a configuration evolution later abandoned. Its colour, recovered: Fossil pigment structures show it was glossy black with an iridescent sheen , like a starling - a rare, specific colour reading from 120 million years ago. Last meals on record: Different specimens preserve a bird, a mammal and a fish in the gut, revealing a versatile small predator that hunted across land, trees and water. Glider, in all likelihood: Its anatomy points to gliding between trees rather than powered flight - a small raptor exploiting the canopy that its larger cousin Deinonychus could not. Microraptor was an agile arboreal hunter that probably glided between trees on its four feathered wings, ambushing birds, mammals and fish - its varied gut contents prove genuine dietary breadth. By taking to the trees it carved out an aerial-and-arboreal niche distinct from ground-running dromaeosaurs, reducing competition with relatives that hunted below. It belongs to the feathered-dinosaur treasure trove of Liaoning, where volcanic ash preserved soft tissue in extraordinary detail. Microraptor shows the origin of flight was a broad experiment , not a single feathered-arm solution - the four-winged design sits alongside Yi qi's membranes and Archaeopteryx's two wings as rival attempts. It demonstrates feathers and aerial locomotion evolving in animals firmly within the dinosaur, not bird, fold. Microraptor reveals that the road to flight branched in directions evolution didn't keep. A four-winged glider with iridescent black feathers shows the bird body plan emerged from a crowded field of experiments, not a tidy straight line.

title: Microraptor — fossil record
text: Hundreds of specimens from the Yixian Formation, many preserving feathers; several individuals show preserved gut contents Evidence level: Exceptional. Liaoning Province, northeastern China Yixian Formation (Jiufotang Formation for some specimens) Material: Multiple skeletons, Feathers, Skull, Limb bones, Skin impressions. Microraptor has one of the largest specimen samples of any Mesozoic feathered theropod, with hundreds of individuals known - a product of the extraordinary preservation in the Yixian Formation where volcanic ash buried animals in fine-grained lake sediments. This abundance has allowed detailed study of feather structure: melanosome analysis has revealed an iridescent structural colouration comparable to a modern starling, an exceptionally specific inference from physical evidence rather than guesswork. Gut contents in different specimens record a lizard, a bird, and a fish - directly evidencing dietary breadth. The four-winged configuration is physically preserved; the aerodynamic modelling of how those wings functioned is where inference enters.

title: Microraptor — How did Microraptor use its four wings?
text: How did Microraptor use its four wings? No living animal flies this way, so the leg-wings' aerodynamic role is reconstructed indirectly. Biplane-style glide (moderate confidence): Leg-wings may have added lift or stability in a tree-to-tree glide. Manoeuvring surfaces (moderate confidence): The hind wings could have aided steering and braking rather than pure lift.

title: Therizinosaurus — overview
text: A large therizinosaurid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, Therizinosaurus belonged to the meat-eating theropod lineage yet evolved into a giant herbivore, with leaf-shaped teeth, a broad barrel torso for fermenting plant matter, and hand claws reaching nearly a metre in length - the longest known of any animal. Those claws were most likely used to hook and pull down vegetation rather than as weapons, though they would also have been formidable in defence against the tyrannosaurids sharing its world. The longest claws in history: Its hand claws reached nearly a metre - the largest known claws of any animal ever , yet wielded by a vegetarian, not a killer. A carnivore's family turned vegan: It belongs to the theropods, Tyrannosaurus's own broad group, yet evolved leaf-shaped teeth and a fermenting gut - a meat-eating lineage that switched sides. A pot-bellied build: A wide pelvis and barrel torso housed a big fermentation vat for plants, utterly unlike the lean, deep-chested frame of its predatory relatives. Tools and deterrents, not weapons: The giant claws most plausibly pulled down branches and warded off predators, like a giant ground sloth's - function inferred from body shape, since the teeth tell the dietary story. Therizinosaurus stood and reached into trees, hooking foliage with its enormous claws, and fermented large volumes of low-quality plants in a capacious gut - a lifestyle convergent with the much later giant ground sloths. In a landscape patrolled by tyrannosaurs, those oversized claws also offered a formidable defence , letting even a slow herbivore deter attack. It shared Cretaceous Asia with armoured and duck-billed herbivores, partitioning the plant supply by feeding high where they fed low. Therizinosaurus is a dramatic case of dietary reversal : a member of the carnivorous theropods that re-evolved herbivory, complete with new teeth, gut and body shape. It shows how flexibly a lineage's basic body plan can be repurposed for an opposite way of life. Therizinosaurus demolishes the idea that ancestry locks in lifestyle. A family of meat-eaters produced a shaggy, pot-bellied, claw-handed vegetarian - proof that evolution constantly recycles old anatomy for entirely new jobs.

title: Therizinosaurus — fossil record
text: Initially only enormous forelimb bones and claws; later material from closely related therizinosaurids (especially Beipiaosaurus and Falcarius) substantially informed reconstructions of the group Evidence level: Fragments. Nemegt Formation exposures, Ömnögovi Province, Mongolia Nemegt Formation Material: Limb bones, Claw. The holotype of Therizinosaurus consists of forelimb elements including the spectacular claws, hind limb fragments, and a few other bones - no skull, no complete torso, no tail. Our confident image of a large, pot-bellied, leaf-eating theropod with metre-long claws comes substantially from better-preserved relatives such as Beipiaosaurus (which preserves feathers and a clear herbivore's gut), Falcarius , and Erlikosaurus . Therizinosaurus itself is inferred to resemble these relatives scaled up - a reasonable inference given shared anatomical features - but the direct fossil evidence for the icon is genuinely thin. The claws are real; nearly everything else in popular reconstructions is extrapolated.

title: Oviraptor — overview
text: A small oviraptorid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, Oviraptor was a short-faced, crested, toothless bird-like dinosaur whose name - meaning 'egg thief' - was based on a misidentification: the eggs it was found near were its own, not stolen. Most of the famous brooding specimens now attributed to oviraptorids formally belong to the close relative Citipati, but the group as a whole has since been confirmed as attentive nesters. Convicted by a coincidence: Found atop a nest, it was named 'egg seizer' on the assumption it was raiding eggs - until later finds revealed the eggs were its own. Caught in the act of brooding: Related specimens sit on nests in a bird-like brooding posture , arms spread over the clutch - direct evidence of parental care in a dinosaur. A toothless, powerful beak: Its deep, toothless beak could crush hard food - possibly shellfish, seeds, or eggs after all - marking it as a flexible omnivore. A crest for showing off: A tall head crest, varying between individuals, most likely served display , echoing the signalling structures seen across dinosaurs from Dilophosaurus to Parasaurolophus. Oviraptor was an agile, feathered omnivore, and oviraptorids as a group brooded their eggs much as a modern bird does - the clearest specimens (formally assigned to the close relative Citipati ) show arms spread over the clutch in a posture identical to brooding birds.
