Feathering was widespread in tyrannosauroids (moderate confidence): Yutyrannus proves the capacity existed in the lineage. Large Late Cretaceous tyrannosaurids were mostly scaly (moderate confidence): Skin impressions from later taxa support scaly regions, though not necessarily the whole body. Mixed covering across body regions and life stages (strong confidence): Different body regions, ages and climates may have carried different coverings - the most cautious reading.

title: Wulong — overview
text: A small microraptorine dromaeosaurid from the Early Cretaceous of Liaoning, China, Wulong was around 0.8 metres long and preserved with a pair of elongated ribbon-like tail feathers that would have functioned in display. The known specimen appears to be a juvenile that already carried these plumes, suggesting display structures developed early in growth rather than only in sexually mature adults. A dragon with plumes: Wulong preserves long feathers on the tail and limbs, adding another spectacular data point to the Jehol feathered-dinosaur record. Young but showy: Histology indicates the known specimen was immature, yet it already carried elaborate plumage that in living birds often appears later. Four-winged context: As a microraptorine, Wulong belongs near the animals that experimented with feathered arms and legs, blurring the line between display and aerodynamics. Growth complicates reconstruction: The fossil warns that a single beautiful specimen may represent a juvenile stage, not the final adult form. Wulong was a small predatory dinosaur in the Jehol ecosystem, likely hunting insects, small vertebrates and other tiny animals around forests and lake margins. Its feathers make it visually dramatic, but its behaviour must be treated cautiously. Feathered limbs may have helped with display, balance, manoeuvring or gliding-like movements in close relatives, but Wulong itself should not be turned into a guaranteed flyer. The most interesting point is developmental: elaborate feathers appeared before full skeletal maturity, suggesting that display biology in early paravians may not map neatly onto modern bird expectations. Wulong helps show that the bird-line neighbourhood was full of evolutionary experiments. Long feathers, tail fans and limb plumage did not emerge in a straight line toward modern birds; they appeared in small predatory dinosaurs with different ecologies, growth patterns and possible display systems. Wulong is not just another feathered dinosaur. It is a reminder that fossils preserve individuals at particular ages, and that the most beautiful specimen may be a teenager rather than an adult blueprint.

title: Wulong — fossil record
text: A complete or near-complete feathered specimen preserving plumage and skeletal anatomy Evidence level: Exceptional. Liaoning Province, China Jiufotang Formation, Jehol Biota Material: Skeleton, Skull, Limb bones, Tail, Feather impressions, Histological evidence. Wulong bohaiensis is based on a beautifully preserved specimen with feather traces and skeletal detail. Its histology indicates immaturity, which makes the fossil especially valuable for studying growth. That same fact complicates reconstruction: adult Wulong may have differed in plumage or proportions. This is a perfect entry for teaching that a fossil is also an individual life stage.

title: Wulong — Were Wulong's long feathers mainly for display or movement?
text: Were Wulong's long feathers mainly for display or movement? It affects how we interpret feather evolution near the origin of birds. Display (moderate confidence): Long tail feathers are visually striking and may have signalled identity or maturity. Aerodynamic role (low confidence): Close relatives used limb feathers in movement, but Wulong's exact ability is not proven. Developmental signal (strong confidence): The specimen's immaturity is central: elaboration before adulthood is a real biological fact preserved in the fossil.

title: Utahraptor — overview
text: A large dromaeosaurid theropod from the Early Cretaceous of Utah, Utahraptor was around 5-6 metres long and one of the biggest raptors known, retaining the enlarged sickle-shaped killing claw on the second toe seen in smaller relatives like Deinonychus and Velociraptor. Its size demonstrates that the dromaeosaurid body plan could scale up substantially without abandoning its defining anatomical features. The raptor scaled up: Utahraptor was far larger than Velociraptor or Deinonychus, bringing the dromaeosaur body plan into big-predator territory . A huge sickle claw: Its enlarged second toe claw shows the classic raptor foot weapon at a scale capable of grappling with sizeable prey. Early in the Cretaceous: Utahraptor lived much earlier than the famous Late Cretaceous raptors, showing large dromaeosaurs were already established early in the period. A tangled fossil block: Important Utahraptor material includes a dense block with multiple individuals, promising insight but requiring careful preparation and interpretation. Utahraptor was a large predatory theropod of the Cedar Mountain ecosystems, probably hunting or scavenging iguanodontian herbivores and other prey. Its sickle claw was likely used in gripping, pinning or cutting during close contact, but it should not be described as a simple slashing knife. As with Deinonychus, claims of pack hunting are tempting but hard to prove. Multiple individuals in the same deposit may reflect social behaviour, a predator trap, drought, flood concentration or other taphonomic processes. The best reading is powerful, close-quarters predator, not movie-style coordinated wolf pack. Utahraptor expands the dromaeosaur story beyond small, agile animals. It shows that the raptor design could be scaled up into a major predatory role and helps connect public fascination with raptors to more careful biomechanical and fossil-evidence questions. Utahraptor gives the public the giant raptor they expected, but the science is more interesting than the movie. The fossil asks how far we can scale a body plan - and how careful we must be before turning a bonebed into a pack.

title: Utahraptor — fossil record
text: Partial skeletons, large claws, cranial and postcranial material, and multi-individual material under study Evidence level: Partial. Utah, USA Cedar Mountain Formation Material: Claws, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Skull fragments, Partial skeletons. Utahraptor is famous, but not known from one perfect mounted skeleton. Its record includes partial but diagnostic material, especially the large dromaeosaurid sickle claw and limb elements. The multi-individual block is potentially important for growth, ecology and behaviour, but its full scientific value depends on preparation and study. The fossil record does not demonstrate wolf-like cooperative hunting.

title: Utahraptor — Did Utahraptor hunt cooperatively?
text: Did Utahraptor hunt cooperatively? The popular image of raptors is deeply social, but fossil evidence rarely proves coordinated behaviour. Social or group behaviour (low confidence): Multi-individual deposits are suggestive but not decisive. Taphonomic accumulation (moderate confidence): Burial processes can gather animals without them living as a pack. Solitary but opportunistic predator (moderate confidence): This remains a safe default for large carnivores without direct behavioural proof.

title: Albertosaurus — overview
text: A tyrannosaurid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Alberta, Albertosaurus was around 9-10 metres long and more slender-built than its close relative Tyrannosaurus, with proportionally longer legs suggesting a faster animal. A bonebed at Dry Island Buffalo Jump containing multiple Albertosaurus individuals of different ages has fuelled ongoing debate over whether tyrannosaurs sometimes travelled or hunted in groups. A tyrannosaur built for pursuit: Compared with T. rex, Albertosaurus was lighter and longer-legged, suggesting a faster predator of northern floodplains. The Dry Island bonebed: A famous deposit preserves many Albertosaurus individuals, making it central to debates over tyrannosaur social behaviour. Not just a small T. rex: Albertosaurus was part of an earlier, slimmer tyrannosaurid radiation, not merely a scaled-down version of the later giant. Barnum Brown's predator: Its discovery history links directly to museum collecting and the fossil-hunting era in Alberta. Albertosaurus was an apex predator of Late Cretaceous Alberta, hunting hadrosaurs, ceratopsians and other animals in a landscape rich with large herbivores. Its lighter build suggests a different predatory style from T. rex: more pursuit-capable, less extreme in skull-crushing specialisation. The Dry Island bonebed is the behavioural centre of the entry. Many individuals in one place may suggest gregariousness or at least group-level ecology, but catastrophic death, drought, flooding or repeated accumulation remain possible. The evidence supports possible social behaviour, not confirmed pack hunting. Albertosaurus shows tyrannosaurids before the final T. rex extreme. It gives a broader view of tyrannosaur evolution: not one inevitable march toward a giant bone-crusher, but a family of predators with different body proportions, ranges and ecologies. Albertosaurus is where tyrannosaur social behaviour becomes a real scientific question rather than a movie trope. It shows how one bonebed can invite big behavioural ideas while still demanding restraint.

title: Albertosaurus — fossil record
text: Multiple specimens including skulls, skeletons and a multi-individual bonebed at Dry Island Buffalo Jump Evidence level: Rich. Alberta, Canada Horseshoe Canyon Formation Material: Skulls, Partial skeletons, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Teeth, Bonebed. Albertosaurus has a strong fossil record by large-theropod standards. The Dry Island bonebed contains multiple individuals and has been central to discussions of tyrannosaur gregariousness. The deposit is behaviourally tempting but taphonomically complex: many animals in one place can mean group life, mass death, carcass concentration or repeated deposition. Its value lies in preserving a population sample, not just a single predator.

title: Albertosaurus — Does the Albertosaurus bonebed prove group living?
text: Does the Albertosaurus bonebed prove group living? It is one of the strongest pieces of evidence used in the tyrannosaur sociality debate. Some gregarious behaviour (moderate confidence): Multiple individuals of different ages together are suggestive. Catastrophic or repeated accumulation (moderate confidence): Taphonomy can gather carcasses without social behaviour. Coordinated pack hunting (low confidence): The fossil record does not demonstrate wolf-like cooperation.

title: Torvosaurus — overview
text: A large megalosaurid theropod from the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation of the western USA and Portugal, Torvosaurus was around 9-10 metres long with robust forelimbs and large serrated teeth, and was one of the biggest predators of its time alongside Allosaurus. Its presence in both North America and Portugal reflects a Jurassic faunal connection between the two regions before the Atlantic widened to break them apart. The other giant of the Morrison: Torvosaurus lived alongside Allosaurus but came from a different theropod lineage, adding predator diversity to the Jurassic. A megalosaurid survivor: It connects back to older Jurassic predators like Megalosaurus, showing a long-running branch of large carnivores. Across the young Atlantic: Closely related Torvosaurus material in Portugal links the Morrison Formation and Lourinhã Formation faunas. Fragmentary but formidable: Its size and skull elements point to a huge predator, but much of its life appearance remains reconstructed from incomplete material. Torvosaurus was a large carnivore in ecosystems already crowded with predatory dinosaurs. It likely hunted or scavenged large herbivores, including sauropods and stegosaurs, but its exact niche relative to Allosaurus remains uncertain. Differences in skull build, tooth form, body size and abundance may indicate different hunting strategies or prey preferences. Unlike Allosaurus, which is known from many individuals, Torvosaurus is rarer, which makes behavioural inference harder. Torvosaurus helps prevent the Jurassic predator story becoming too Allosaurus-centred. It shows that multiple large theropod lineages overlapped, with megalosaurids persisting as major predators while allosauroids diversified. Torvosaurus is the antidote to one-apex-predator storytelling. The Jurassic was not ruled by a single carnivore; it was a dangerous ecosystem with several ways to be a giant meat-eater.

title: Torvosaurus — fossil record
text: Partial skull and postcranial material from the Morrison Formation and related material in Portugal Evidence level: Partial. Colorado, Wyoming, Utah, USA; Lourinhã region, Portugal Morrison Formation; Lourinhã Formation Material: Skull elements, Teeth, Limb bones, Vertebrae, Partial skeleton. Torvosaurus is well enough known to establish it as a major megalosaurid predator, but its fossil record is patchier than Allosaurus. Portuguese material has been interpreted as a close relative or separate species, showing faunal similarity between western North America and Europe during the Late Jurassic. The animal's size is clear; finer details of behaviour are inferred from partial anatomy and ecological context.

title: Torvosaurus — How did Torvosaurus share ecosystems with Allosaurus?
text: How did Torvosaurus share ecosystems with Allosaurus? Predator diversity reveals how Jurassic food webs were structured. Different prey or feeding strategy (moderate confidence): Anatomy and rarity suggest niche separation. Direct competition (moderate confidence): Both were large predators in overlapping environments. Regional replacement (low confidence): Portuguese and North American records complicate the picture.

title: Dreadnoughtus — overview
text: A large titanosaur sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia, Dreadnoughtus is known from one of the most complete titanosaur skeletons yet found, making it a primary reference point for the body proportions of giant sauropods. Mass estimates derived from those bones have varied substantially between analyses, illustrating how difficult it is to translate bone geometry into body weight for animals with no living counterparts of comparable size. A giant with much of the skeleton known: Dreadnoughtus is among the most complete very large titanosaurs, making it unusually informative for an animal of such size. Still growing: Bone histology suggests the type individual had not finished growing, meaning its adult size may have been even greater. A famous mass debate: The original high mass estimate was challenged by later volumetric work, making Dreadnoughtus a case study in weighing extinct giants . Titanosaurs at scale: It shows that Cretaceous sauropods were not fading everywhere; in South America, titanosaurs were still producing colossal bodies. Dreadnoughtus was a huge browsing herbivore of Cretaceous Patagonia, moving through floodplain environments and consuming immense quantities of plant matter. Its size would have made adults difficult prey, though juveniles were vulnerable. The more interesting ecological question is not just 'how big was it?' but how giant titanosaurs sustained themselves: wide-ranging foraging, efficient digestion, rapid growth and low chewing investment all mattered. Its death and burial also matter: preserving so much of such a large animal requires unusual taphonomic circumstances. Dreadnoughtus is a flagship for modern sauropod science because it combines size with measurable completeness. Instead of extrapolating from scraps, researchers could use major limb and body elements - and then argue about how those bones translate into soft tissue and mass. Dreadnoughtus teaches that 'biggest dinosaur' headlines are less important than method. The fossil is magnificent because it lets scientists test how they estimate giants, not because it gives one final number.

title: Dreadnoughtus — fossil record
text: Two individuals, including an exceptionally complete giant titanosaur skeleton lacking much of the skull and neck Evidence level: Rich. Santa Cruz Province, Patagonia, Argentina Cerro Fortaleza Formation Material: Limb bones, Vertebrae, Ribs, Girdle elements, Tail vertebrae, Partial skeleton. Dreadnoughtus schrani is unusually complete for a gigantic titanosaur, preserving much of the postcranial skeleton. The skull and portions of the neck are missing, but the limb and body material allow unusually direct estimates of proportions. Histology indicates the type specimen was still growing. The mass estimate is deliberately written as a range because published methods disagree: this disagreement is the scientific point, not a weakness to hide.

title: Dreadnoughtus — How heavy was Dreadnoughtus really?
text: How heavy was Dreadnoughtus really? Mass estimates shape everything from metabolism to ecology and biomechanics. Extremely high mass near 60 tonnes (moderate confidence): Based on limb-scaling methods applied to the original description. Lower mass around 30-40 tonnes (strong confidence): Volumetric reassessment produced lower, better-constrained estimates. A range rather than a number (strong confidence): Body mass should be presented as debated, not fixed - the disagreement is the scientific point.

title: Nanuqsaurus — overview
text: A tyrannosaurine theropod from the latest Cretaceous of the North Slope of Alaska, Nanuqsaurus was a relatively small tyrannosaur estimated at around 5-6 metres - considerably smaller than Tyrannosaurus - living in a high-latitude environment that experienced months of polar darkness each year. Known from limited material, it is notable as evidence that tyrannosaurs occupied arctic ecosystems and likely had physiological or behavioural adaptations to extreme seasonal conditions. The polar tyrant: Nanuqsaurus comes from the Prince Creek Formation of Alaska, a high-latitude ecosystem with long winter darkness. Small, but uncertain: It was originally interpreted as relatively small for a tyrannosaur, though size estimates are limited by fragmentary material. Arctic ecology, not icy wasteland: Its world was warmer than modern Arctic Alaska, but still seasonally extreme. A predator in the dark: Nanuqsaurus adds a carnivore to the evidence that polar dinosaur ecosystems were complete, resident communities. Nanuqsaurus was probably a top predator in the Prince Creek ecosystem, hunting hadrosaurs, small ornithischians and other high-latitude animals. Its ecology is less well known than T. rex or Albertosaurus because the fossil material is limited, but its setting is powerful. For months each year, its environment would have been dark, cool and seasonal. Whether it relied on keen smell, low-light vision, scavenging, or year-round resident prey communities remains open. The environment is the story: tyrannosaurs were not confined to lush temperate floodplains. Nanuqsaurus helps make polar dinosaur science concrete. It links tyrannosaur evolution with high-latitude ecology and supports the broader view that many dinosaurs had physiology and behaviours capable of handling seasonal extremes. Nanuqsaurus turns the Arctic into dinosaur country. It makes the polar dinosaur story less abstract: in the winter dark, there were predators too.

title: Nanuqsaurus — fossil record
text: Fragmentary cranial material attributed to a tyrannosaurine Evidence level: Fragments. North Slope, Alaska, USA Prince Creek Formation Material: Skull fragments, Maxilla/nasal/frontal material, Associated tyrannosaurid material from the formation. Nanuqsaurus hoglundi is based on limited skull material, so every reconstruction must be cautious. The taxon is important less for completeness than for context: it documents a derived tyrannosaurine in a polar ecosystem. Size, ecology and exact behaviour are therefore more uncertain than in better-known tyrannosaurs. Its strongest evidence value is biogeographic and palaeoecological.

title: Nanuqsaurus — Was Nanuqsaurus genuinely small because of polar ecology?
text: Was Nanuqsaurus genuinely small because of polar ecology? It affects claims about food limitation and Arctic adaptation in tyrannosaurs. Small-bodied polar tyrannosaur (moderate confidence): The original material suggests a smaller animal. Size uncertain due to fragmentary fossils (strong confidence): Limited material means caution is essential. Ecological adaptation to polar conditions (low confidence): Plausible, but not directly proven from the available evidence.

title: Hatzegopteryx — overview
text: An azhdarchid pterosaur from the Late Cretaceous Haţeg Basin of Romania, Hatzegopteryx had an estimated wingspan of 10-12 metres and a massively built skull and reinforced neck bones that distinguish it from other azhdarchids of comparable size. It lived on an island ecosystem populated by dwarf dinosaurs, making it likely the apex predator of that environment - a flying reptile filling a role usually occupied by large theropods. A giant on an island: Hatzegopteryx lived in the Haţeg ecosystem, famous for island-dwarfed dinosaurs and unusual Late Cretaceous fauna. Built more robustly than expected: Neck and skull material suggest a stockier, stronger animal than the standard long-necked azhdarchid template. A pterosaur as land predator: Rather than skimming oceans, azhdarchids are now often interpreted as ground-stalking predators of small animals. The top predator problem: In Haţeg, Hatzegopteryx may have been one of the few animals large enough to dominate sizeable prey. Hatzegopteryx was likely a terrestrial stalker, walking on all fours across island floodplains and woodlands, seizing small dinosaurs, mammals, reptiles and carrion with a huge beak. It could fly, but its ecological role may have been closer to a giant ground hornbill or marabou stork than a seabird. Its robust neck matters because it challenges the idea that all giant azhdarchids were built the same. In a landscape of dwarf sauropods and ornithopods, a towering pterosaur may have been a dominant predator. Note: Hatzegopteryx was a pterosaur, not a dinosaur - it belongs to a separate reptile lineage. Hatzegopteryx expands the pterosaur story beyond flight. It shows that pterosaurs were not simply aerial side characters next to dinosaurs; some may have been major land predators shaping island ecosystems. Hatzegopteryx makes pterosaurs feel dangerous again - not as monsters, but as real ecological players. It turns the sky reptile into a land predator towering over island dinosaurs.

title: Hatzegopteryx — fossil record
text: Fragmentary but distinctive skull and neck material from giant azhdarchid pterosaurs Evidence level: Fragments. Haţeg Basin, Transylvania, Romania Densuş-Ciula Formation and related Haţeg deposits Material: Skull fragments, Jaw material, Cervical vertebrae, Limb fragments. Hatzegopteryx is based on fragmentary remains, but the preserved skull and neck bones are unusually robust. Biomechanical work on the neck supports the idea of a shorter, stronger cervical system than in some other azhdarchids. Because the fossil is incomplete, wingspan and mass estimates are ranges rather than fixed numbers. Its importance lies in ecology and biomechanics, not completeness.

title: Hatzegopteryx — How predatory was Hatzegopteryx on land?
text: How predatory was Hatzegopteryx on land? It changes how pterosaurs fit into dinosaur-dominated ecosystems. Terrestrial apex or mesopredator (moderate confidence): Its robust skull and neck support strong feeding performance. Generalist scavenger/predator (strong confidence): Azhdarchids likely exploited many food sources - the cautious reading. Pure aerial fisher (low confidence): This older image fits poorly with current azhdarchid anatomy.

title: Elasmosaurus — overview
text: An elasmosaurid plesiosaur from the Late Cretaceous Western Interior Seaway, Elasmosaurus was around 10-11 metres long with an extraordinary neck of over 70 vertebrae comprising roughly half its total body length, and a small head at the end of it. Edward Drinker Cope's original reconstruction famously placed the head on the tail end; its correction by rival O.C. Marsh became one of the more memorable episodes in the 19th-century 'Bone Wars.' The neck that fooled Cope: Edward Drinker Cope famously reconstructed the animal with its head on the wrong end, mistaking its extraordinary neck for a tail. A sea reptile, not a dinosaur: Elasmosaurus lived alongside dinosaurs but belonged to a separate marine reptile lineage - plesiosaurs were not dinosaurs. Extreme neck evolution: Its neck contained an astonishing number of vertebrae, showing how far plesiosaurs pushed body-plan specialisation. The Bone Wars lesson: The mistake became part of the rivalry between Cope and Marsh, but the deeper lesson is about fragmentary anatomy and scientific correction. Elasmosaurus swam through the Western Interior Seaway, feeding on fish, squid-like animals and other small marine prey. Its long neck is often imagined as a flexible snake-like weapon, but plesiosaur necks were probably more limited than old artworks suggest. The animal likely used stealth, reach and body positioning rather than wildly whipping its head around. It was one of many marine reptiles in a seaway that split North America, sharing those waters with mosasaurs, fish, turtles, ammonites and toothed birds. Elasmosaurus is useful because it teaches two lessons at once: marine reptiles evolved body plans as specialised and strange as anything on land, and scientific errors can become productive when corrected. Its wrong-end reconstruction is famous, but its real importance lies in the anatomy that made the mistake possible. Elasmosaurus is the perfect fossil humility story. Cope's mistake is funny, but the animal's body was genuinely hard to interpret. The correction is not an embarrassment to science - it is science working.

title: Elasmosaurus — fossil record
text: Incomplete type skeleton including extensive neck vertebrae, partial skull and postcranial material Evidence level: Partial. Kansas, USA Pierre Shale Material: Cervical vertebrae, Partial skull elements, Limb material, Vertebrae, Gastroliths. Elasmosaurus platyurus is historically important but not completely preserved. The type material includes enough of the neck and body to establish its extreme proportions, but missing and scattered elements contributed to early reconstruction problems. Later redescription clarified its anatomy. Its fossil record should be understood as scientifically rich but historically messy. Elasmosaurus was a marine reptile, not a dinosaur.

title: Elasmosaurus — What was the long neck for?
text: What was the long neck for? Elasmosaurus is the classic extreme-neck plesiosaur, but function is not obvious. Stealthy prey approach (moderate confidence): A small head on a long neck may have reduced disturbance near prey. Wide feeding reach (moderate confidence): The neck increased strike area without moving the whole body. Highly flexible swan-like neck (low confidence): Modern studies generally reject extreme flexibility in elasmosaurid necks.

title: Postosuchus — overview
text: A large rauisuchian archosaur from the Late Triassic of Texas, Postosuchus was around 4-5 metres long and not a dinosaur but a member of the pseudosuchian - crocodile-relative - lineage that dominated large-predator niches before dinosaurs came to the fore. Its upright posture, deep skull, and serrated teeth give it a superficially theropod-like appearance, illustrating how similar predatory body plans can evolve independently in unrelated lineages. Not a dinosaur: Postosuchus belonged to the crocodile side of the archosaur family tree (Pseudosuchia), making it a crucial comparison for early dinosaurs. A Triassic top predator: Before theropods dominated, pseudosuchians like Postosuchus were among the major land carnivores. Convergence in action: Its erect limbs, big skull and predatory build can look dinosaur-like, but they evolved on a different archosaur branch. Dinosaurs began as underdogs: Postosuchus helps explain why early dinosaurs were only one part of a broader Triassic experiment in ruling land. Postosuchus was a large carnivore in Late Triassic ecosystems, hunting early dinosaurs, dicynodonts, small reptiles and other animals. Its powerful jaws and serrated teeth point to a predator capable of tackling sizeable prey. It may have been mostly quadrupedal or capable of some bipedal postures depending on interpretation, but it should not be presented as a tyrannosaur ancestor or a true dinosaur. It belonged to Pseudosuchia - the crocodile-line archosaurs - and its ecological dominance makes the Triassic feel very different from the Jurassic. Postosuchus is one of the best entries for teaching archosaur evolution. It shows that dinosaurs did not simply appear and immediately take over. During the Late Triassic, many crocodile-line relatives occupied the large-predator roles later filled by theropods. Postosuchus is the animal that stops DinoDex from becoming dinosaur propaganda. It shows that dinosaurs inherited the Earth only after a wider archosaur world collapsed around them.

title: Postosuchus — fossil record
text: Skull and postcranial material from the Dockum Group of Texas, with additional related material from North America Evidence level: Rich. Post Quarry, Garza County, Texas, USA; additional North American material Cooper Canyon Formation, Dockum Group Material: Skull, Partial skeletons, Vertebrae, Limb bones, Teeth, Pelvic material. Postosuchus kirkpatricki is known from relatively good material for a Triassic pseudosuchian, including skull and postcranial remains. Its classification has shifted as archosaur relationships have been revised. It was once linked loosely to dinosaur ancestry in older literature, but modern interpretation places it firmly on the crocodile-line side of Archosauria. This entry actively corrects the 'dinosaur-like means dinosaur' mistake.

title: Postosuchus — How should Postosuchus be reconstructed and classified?
text: How should Postosuchus be reconstructed and classified? It affects how we picture Triassic locomotion and predator ecology. Primarily quadrupedal pseudosuchian predator (moderate confidence): Modern reconstructions generally favour this posture. Facultatively bipedal at times (low confidence): Historically suggested but debated; not the current consensus. Dinosaur ancestor or dinosaur relative (low confidence): This is outdated: modern systematics firmly places Postosuchus on the crocodile-line side of Archosauria.

title: Psittacosaurus — overview
text: A basal ceratopsian ornithischian from the Early Cretaceous of Mongolia and wider Asia, Psittacosaurus was around 2 metres long with a distinctive deep parrot-like beak and no true horns - an early and relatively unspecialised member of the lineage that would later produce Triceratops. Exceptionally preserved specimens provide direct evidence of skin texture, a counter-shaded colour pattern, tail bristles, and even the cloaca, making it one of the most completely understood non-avian dinosaurs in terms of soft-tissue anatomy. A ceratopsian before the frills: Psittacosaurus was an early relative of Triceratops, but lacked the giant horns and frill of later ceratopsids - a reminder that iconic features take time to evolve . Colour from the skin: Exceptional specimens preserve pigment patterns showing countershading, one of the strongest dinosaur colour reconstructions outside feathered theropods. Tail bristles, not full fluff: Some specimens preserve long bristle-like structures on the tail, adding nuance to the ornithischian filament debate. A fossil record of growth: Many specimens across ages make Psittacosaurus important for studying development, posture, diet and social behaviour. Psittacosaurus was a small, agile herbivore or low-browser feeding with a sharp beak and cheek teeth. It likely ate tough plants, seeds and other low vegetation, possibly aided by gastroliths. Its colour pattern suggests camouflage in a habitat with directional light, and its tail bristles may have been visual display structures. The animal's abundance makes it one of the best windows into early ceratopsian biology. It was not a miniature Triceratops; it was a different stage in the ceratopsian experiment, before horns and frills became the headline. Psittacosaurus links several DinoDex themes at once: ceratopsian origins, colour reconstruction, skin preservation, filament-like structures and growth. It is one of the most scientifically useful small dinosaurs because it shows how much information exceptional preservation can add beyond bones. Psittacosaurus is a small dinosaur with a huge scientific footprint. It proves that the right fossil can reveal not just bones, but colour, skin, growth and the visual life of an extinct animal.

title: Psittacosaurus — fossil record
text: Hundreds of specimens, including juveniles, adults, articulated skeletons, skin impressions, bristle-like tail structures and exceptional soft-tissue preservation Evidence level: Exceptional. Mongolia, northern China and other parts of Asia Multiple Early Cretaceous units including Yixian/Jehol-related deposits and equivalents Material: Skull, Skeletons, Growth series, Skin impressions, Pigment evidence, Tail bristles, Gastroliths. Psittacosaurus has one of the richest fossil records of any small dinosaur. Its exceptional specimens preserve skin and pigment patterns, enabling countershading reconstructions. Tail bristles show unusual integument in an ornithischian, but these should not be treated as direct equivalents of bird feathers without caution. The abundance of specimens also allows study of growth and variation, making it a rare dinosaur where individual biology and population-level questions overlap.

title: Psittacosaurus — What were the tail bristles and colour patterns for?
text: What were the tail bristles and colour patterns for? Psittacosaurus sits at the intersection of skin, display, camouflage and feather-origin debates. Countershading for camouflage (strong confidence): Pigment distribution supports this interpretation. Tail bristles for display (moderate confidence): Their position and form fit signalling, but direct behaviour is unobserved. Evidence for widespread ornithischian feathers (low confidence): The bristles are important but not enough to make all ornithischians fluffy.

title: Borealopelta — overview
text: A nodosaurid ankylosaur from the Early Cretaceous of Alberta, Borealopelta markmitchelli is the most extraordinarily preserved large dinosaur ever found - skin, armour plates in life position, keratin sheaths and stomach contents all intact in three dimensions. At around 5.5 metres and over a tonne, it was a heavily built low-browsing herbivore with extensive lateral armour and large parascapular shoulder spines. Its preserved melanosomes show a reddish-brown countershaded coat, making it the first large dinosaur for which actual colour and pattern are directly evidenced rather than inferred. The closest thing to a photograph of a living dinosaur: The Suncor specimen preserves skin, osteoderms, keratin sheaths and stomach contents in three dimensions . It sank upside-down to a marine seafloor shortly after death, and fine sediment buried it before decay could destroy the soft tissue. Most large dinosaur fossils are disarticulated fragments; this one looks as though it died yesterday. A one-tonne animal hiding from something: Melanosomes preserved in the skin show a reddish-brown countershaded coat - darker above, lighter below - a camouflage pattern. No living land animal above roughly 500 kg is countershaded. That Borealopelta was, despite its armour, tells us the predators of its environment were formidable enough that concealment was still worth investing in. Last meal, dated to the season: Stomach contents were dominated by leptosporangiate fern leaves - around 85% of the leaf fraction, which itself made up 88% of total stomach mass - with cycads at only 3% and trace conifers. Crucially, Borealopelta was eating just one type of fern while ignoring others available nearby, suggesting selective feeding . Charcoal in the contents places it in recently burned landscape. Growth rings in a preserved twig allowed researchers to date death to late spring to mid-summer . Washed out to sea: The specimen came from marine sediment far from the nearest Cretaceous shoreline, consistent with a carcass transported by river then floating at sea before sinking - a taphonomic route called 'bloat and float' . Marine burial is more rapid and less disturbed than terrestrial, which is what produced the preservation. Borealopelta was a selective low browser, its stomach contents showing it actively chose certain ferns over others available in a recovering post-fire landscape. Its broad, heavily armoured body would have been slow but well-defended. The countershading result adds something unexpected: for all its passive armour, this animal also invested in not being seen . Whether it was solitary or moved in groups is unknown - the specimen is a single individual and no population data exists for this species. Nodosaurids were one of the two main ankylosaur lineages, retaining lateral spines but lacking the tail club of the ankylosaurids. Borealopelta's large parascapular spines and extensive armour represent the group near its most heavily invested in passive defence. Phylogenetically it sits close to Pawpawsaurus and Europelta within Nodosauridae. Borealopelta is the closest palaeontology has come to a photograph of a living dinosaur - and the countershading result reshaped assumptions about Early Cretaceous predator-prey dynamics, because if a heavily armoured megaherbivore was still hiding, its predators were clearly more dangerous than the armour alone could address.

title: Borealopelta — fossil record
text: Single nearly complete specimen (holotype TMP 2011.033.0001), Royal Tyrrell Museum of Palaeontology, Drumheller, Alberta Evidence level: Exceptional. Millennium Mine (Suncor Energy), Fort McMurray, Alberta, Canada Wabiskaw Member, Clearwater Formation Material: Nearly complete articulated skeleton, Skin and osteoderms in life position, Keratin sheaths on armour, Stomach contents (cololite), Preserved melanosomes. The holotype is one of the most complete and best-preserved large dinosaur specimens ever recovered, found in marine sediment consistent with post-mortem transport from a terrestrial environment. Six years of preparation by Mark Mitchell preceded formal description; the species name honours him. Countershading evidence was published with the formal description in Current Biology (Brown et al., 2017). Stomach contents were analysed separately in Royal Society Open Science (Brown, Greenwood et al., 2020), identifying selective feeding on leptosporangiate ferns, charcoal from a burned landscape, and growth ring evidence for death in late spring to mid-summer.

title: Borealopelta — Was countershading in a 1,300 kg armoured animal genuinely for camouflage?
text: Was countershading in a 1,300 kg armoured animal genuinely for camouflage? No living animal of comparable mass is countershaded, making the inference unusual even though the pigment evidence is clear. Camouflage under genuine predation pressure (moderate confidence): The pigment distribution matches camouflage function precisely, and the large theropods of the Albian fauna were capable of attacking even well-armoured megaherbivores. The authors' own conclusion in Current Biology (Brown et al., 2017). Display or thermoregulation (low confidence): Alternative functions for pigment patterning cannot be fully excluded, but the countershaded distribution specifically matches concealment rather than display patterns.

title: Tenontosaurus — overview
text: A medium to large ornithopod from the Early Cretaceous of western North America, Tenontosaurus tilletti was a facultatively bipedal herbivore reaching 6.5 to 8 metres, best known for its unusually long tail stiffened by a dense network of ossified tendons and for its repeated association in the fossil record with the dromaeosaurid Deinonychus. That association - multiple Deinonychus individuals found with Tenontosaurus carcasses - generated the pack-hunting hypothesis that shaped how the public and scientists alike thought about raptor behaviour for decades. The animal that launched the pack-hunting hypothesis: Ostrom noticed that multiple Deinonychus individuals were repeatedly found with Tenontosaurus carcasses. A single Deinonychus at roughly 70-100 kg could not plausibly kill a one-tonne Tenontosaurus alone, so he proposed cooperative pack hunting . The inference was vivid and directly influenced both the Dinosaur Renaissance and Jurassic Park's portrayal of raptors as coordinated hunters. The same evidence, reread: A 2007 reanalysis by Roach and Brinkman argued the sites more likely record competitive feeding aggregations of uncooperative individuals drawn to the same carcass - closer to Komodo dragon feeding frenzies than wolf packs. Evidence of probable intraspecific aggression among Deinonychus at one site, and the behaviour of living diapsids, led them to conclude cooperative hunting was the least parsimonious reading. Isotopes shift the picture further: Frederickson et al.'s 2020 stable isotope study of Deinonychus teeth found that juveniles and adults had different diets - the carbon-13 signal of large teeth matches Tenontosaurus, but the dietary shift across age classes mirrors asocial reptiles like crocodilians, not pack-hunting canids where young are provisioned by adults. The data add to a growing case that Deinonychus was not a complex social hunter by mammalian standards. A common animal, not a famous one: With over sixty specimens from a wide geographic range, Tenontosaurus was clearly abundant. It is not famous in its own right, but it is the prey context that makes the most-debated behavioural claim in dromaeosaur science legible - without Tenontosaurus, the Deinonychus association has nothing to associate with. Tenontosaurus was probably facultatively bipedal, moving quadrupedally when feeding but capable of bipedal locomotion. Its stiffened tail, ossified by a dense tendon network, was unusually long even for an ornithopod of its size and may have aided balance. Whether it aggregated socially is unknown. Its abundance across multiple formations implies a wide and successful ecological range, and shed Deinonychus teeth at Tenontosaurus sites confirm the predator-prey relationship. What the association records about the nature of that predation remains disputed. Tenontosaurus sits phylogenetically between the small basal ornithopods and the larger iguanodontians and hadrosaurs. Bone histology studies place it as a useful reference point for understanding how growth rates evolved across ornithopods as the group scaled toward hadrosaur body sizes - a position intermediate in both size and skeletal grade. Tenontosaurus is the animal through which palaeontology has been forced to confront what taphonomic association actually proves about predator behaviour - and how quickly a vivid, coherent story can outrun the evidence that produced it.

title: Tenontosaurus — fossil record
text: Over 60 specimens from the Cloverly Formation (Montana, Wyoming) and Antlers Formation (Oklahoma, Texas) Evidence level: Good. Montana, Wyoming, Oklahoma, Texas, USA Cloverly Formation; Antlers Formation Material: Multiple partial and complete skeletons, Skulls, Ossified tail tendons, Limb bones. The type specimens were collected during Ostrom's Yale Peabody Museum expeditions of 1964-66 and described in the same 1970 monograph as Deinonychus. Approximately one fifth of known Tenontosaurus specimens are found in association with Deinonychus remains or shed teeth; most associated Tenontosaurus are sub-adult individuals. The pack-hunting hypothesis was proposed by Ostrom (1969-70) and challenged by Roach and Brinkman (2007). Frederickson, Engel and Cifelli (2020) added stable isotope evidence against cooperative social hunting in Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology (doi:10.1016/j.palaeo.2020.109780).

title: Tenontosaurus — Did Deinonychus hunt Tenontosaurus cooperatively, or is the association a competitive feeding aggregation?
text: Did Deinonychus hunt Tenontosaurus cooperatively, or is the association a competitive feeding aggregation? A single Deinonychus was too small to take an adult Tenontosaurus alone, making multiple individuals the minimum required - but multiple individuals at a carcass does not require coordination. Cooperative pack hunting (low confidence): Originally proposed by Ostrom (1969-70); now considered unparsimonious relative to the behaviour of living diapsids. Most associated Tenontosaurus are sub-adult individuals, within reach of a single large Deinonychus. Competitive feeding aggregation (moderate confidence): Multiple individuals drawn to a carcass and competing, possibly turning on one another. Better matches known crocodilian and varanid behaviour. Supported by Roach and Brinkman (2007) and the isotope work of Frederickson et al. (2020, Palaeogeography Palaeoclimatology Palaeoecology).

title: Centrosaurus — overview
text: A centrosaurine ceratopsid from the Campanian of Alberta, Centrosaurus apertus was a medium-sized horned dinosaur around 6 metres long, with a single large nasal horn and an elaborate frill bearing paired curved hooks at its upper margin. It is known from at least eight large monodominant bonebeds in the Dinosaur Park Formation - a scale of death assemblage unmatched by almost any other non-avian dinosaur - making it the primary fossil case for large-scale herding behaviour in the group. Eight bonebeds and a mega-site: At least eight large paucispecific Centrosaurus bonebeds are documented in Dinosaur Provincial Park alone. The Hilda mega-bonebed, extending over a wide area of the Oldman Formation, produced bone density estimates implying thousands of individuals dying in a single event - interpreted as mass mortality from seasonal tropical storm flooding, not a continuous accumulation. A crowd in death is not a herd in life: Taphonomic analysis of the Dinosaur Park bonebeds identified the deposits as lag accumulations at palaeochannel bases, with disarticulated and abraded bone consistent with flood transport. The consistency of this pattern across multiple independent sites is striking, but aggregation at death does not require year-round social structure - animals that are normally solitary can mass at water sources independently and die together. Horn and frill through ontogeny: Multiple bonebeds preserve Centrosaurus across a full range of growth stages. Study of horn and frill morphology across these individuals shows that the ornamentation changed substantially through ontogeny - a juvenile's frill looks markedly different from an adult's, which has consequences for both taxonomy and attempts to read function from adult ornamentation alone. Albertosaurus teeth in the mix: Several Centrosaurus bonebeds contain shed teeth of the tyrannosaurid Albertosaurus alongside the ceratopsid material. Whether Albertosaurus drove animals into the water or simply scavenged the aftermath is unknown, but it places predator presence directly in the bonebed record . The scale and consistency of the bonebed record is the strongest available argument for large-scale gregariousness in any non-avian dinosaur. Multiple independent sites, consistent taphonomy, and a geographic spread suggesting movement across the Campanian coastal plain of Alberta all support a picture of Centrosaurus living in large groups . The function of the nasal horn and frill remains debated between intraspecific display and species recognition. Direct combat using the horn is possible but not confirmed by healed trauma evidence. Centrosaurus belongs to Centrosaurinae, the short-frilled ceratopsids, closely related to Styracosaurus and Pachyrhinosaurus. Centrosaurine genera replace each other stratigraphically through the Campanian of Alberta over a geologically short interval - a pattern that may reflect rapid evolutionary turnover or geographic variation sampled at different time horizons. Centrosaurus is the strongest available fossil case for large-scale herding in non-avian dinosaurs - and the bonebed record also shows precisely why taphonomy has to be interrogated before the obvious interpretation is accepted.

title: Centrosaurus — fossil record
text: At least eight large monodominant bonebeds in the Dinosaur Park Formation; additional sites in the Oldman Formation including the Hilda mega-bonebed; multiple articulated skulls and partial skeletons Evidence level: Rich. Dinosaur Provincial Park and Hilda area, Alberta, Canada Dinosaur Park Formation; Oldman Formation Material: Multiple monodominant bonebeds, Articulated skulls, Partial skeletons, Material spanning full ontogenetic range. Bone bed 43 in Dinosaur Provincial Park was one of the first Centrosaurus sites to receive detailed taphonomic analysis (Ryan, Russell, Eberth and Currie, 2001, PALAIOS). The Hilda mega-bonebed was subsequently described and interpreted as evidence for herds potentially numbering in the thousands moving seasonally across the coastal plain. Shed Albertosaurus teeth are reported from several bonebeds. The genus is one of the taxonomically best-supported ceratopsids, with abundant material across growth stages enabling study of ontogenetic change in horn and frill morphology.

title: Centrosaurus — Do the bonebeds confirm herding behaviour, or only that Centrosaurus aggregated at death?
text: Do the bonebeds confirm herding behaviour, or only that Centrosaurus aggregated at death? Mass death events can draw normally solitary animals together, so the taphonomic pattern alone cannot distinguish year-round herding from temporary aggregation. Large herding animal with seasonal migration (moderate confidence): Multiple independent bonebeds with consistent taphonomy across a wide area is difficult to explain without genuine gregariousness. The Hilda mega-bonebed scale is particularly hard to account for with temporary drought aggregation alone. Seasonal aggregation, not permanent herding (low confidence): Animals drawn independently to water sources during storms could die in large numbers without ever forming year-round social groups. The fossil record cannot distinguish these scenarios directly.

title: Patagotitan — overview
text: A giant titanosaur from the Albian of Patagonia, Patagotitan mayorum is one of the most complete giant sauropods known and a serious candidate for the largest land animal yet described, with remains of at least six individuals from a single quarry in Chubut Province, Argentina. Its estimated length of around 37 metres and mass somewhere between 44 and 70 tonnes - the range itself the result of different estimation methods giving strikingly different answers - makes it the clearest illustration in the fossil record of how much the question 'how big was it?' depends on which method you trust. How do you weigh a dinosaur?: The 2017 description produced two different mass figures: 69 tonnes from a limb-bone scaling equation and a range of 44-78 tonnes from volumetric 3D modelling. These are not minor rounding differences - the lower volumetric bound is barely half the scaling estimate. The choice of method is the answer, and the field has not settled which is more reliable at extreme body sizes. Is it actually the largest?: Argentinosaurus, known from less complete material, has dorsal vertebrae and a sacral series that are substantially larger than Patagotitan's in direct comparison. Paul's 2019 analysis estimated Argentinosaurus at 65-75 tonnes. The 'largest land animal' title for Patagotitan is a reasonable claim given the completeness of its material, but it is not settled against all competitors. Six individuals, one quarry: Material from at least six separate individuals was recovered across three burial horizons at a single site - not a bonebed in the ceratopsian sense, but suggesting that multiple animals used or died near the same area over time, perhaps drawn by water or following the same corridor across the Patagonian landscape. A Lognkosaurian size event: Patagotitan belongs to Lognkosauria, a clade within titanosaurs that produced several of the largest known sauropods, all from South America. The group represents a genuine evolutionary size event within titanosaurs - a subset that pushed body size dramatically beyond related lineages, the drivers of which remain an active research question. Like all titanosaurs, Patagotitan was a quadrupedal browser with a long neck giving access to vegetation out of reach of smaller animals. At its estimated size, healthy adults would have had few predators, though the large carcharodontosaurid theropods known from equivalent-aged Patagonian formations would have been capable of taking juveniles. No direct evidence of social behaviour exists beyond the multi-individual quarry, which may reflect taphonomic circumstances rather than gregariousness. Titanosaurs replaced diplodocids and brachiosaurids as the dominant sauropods globally through the Cretaceous. Within titanosaurs, Lognkosauria occupies the extreme of the body size distribution. Whether the gigantism in this group reflects dietary opportunity, predator escape, or developmental factors is actively studied. Patagotitan is the most complete giant titanosaur yet described, and the clearest illustration of a deceptively simple question - how big was this animal? - being, in practice, a question about which method you trust.

title: Patagotitan — fossil record
text: Remains of at least six individuals from a single quarry at La Flecha ranch; holotype MPEF-PV 3400 includes vertebrae, ribs, sternal plates, scapulocoracoid, femora and pubes Evidence level: Good. La Flecha ranch, Chubut Province, Patagonia, Argentina Candeleros Formation Material: Cervical and dorsal vertebrae, Caudal vertebrae, Dorsal ribs, Sternal plates, Scapulocoracoid, Femora, Pubes. Formally named in Proceedings of the Royal Society B (Carballido, Pol, Otero, Cerda, Salgado, Garrido, Ramezani, Cuneo and Krause, 2017). Six individuals are represented across three burial horizons at a single site. A skeletal mount is on display at the American Museum of Natural History, New York. Mass estimates in the published literature range from approximately 44 to 77 tonnes depending on method, with the original description's scaling equation producing 69 tonnes and Gregory Paul's 2019 volumetric analysis suggesting 50-55 tonnes.

title: Patagotitan — Was Patagotitan the largest land animal, and how reliable are its mass estimates?
text: Was Patagotitan the largest land animal, and how reliable are its mass estimates? Mass estimation from incomplete fossil material produces different results depending on method, and the main competitors for the title are known from even less material. Largest known land animal, approximately 69 tonnes (moderate confidence): The scaling equation result from Carballido et al. (2017, Proceedings of the Royal Society B). Widely cited but subject to known problems with limb-bone scaling at extreme body sizes. Argentinosaurus was probably larger (low confidence): Direct skeletal comparison of dorsal vertebrae favours Argentinosaurus, but its mass cannot be reliably estimated from available material. The question may be unresolvable without better specimens. 40-55 tonnes is more defensible (moderate confidence): Paul (2019) and several volumetric analyses converge on lower figures. The range across published estimates is wide enough that any single number should be treated with caution.

title: Effigia — overview
text: A pseudosuchian archosaur from the Late Triassic of New Mexico, Effigia okeeffeae was a member of the crocodile line that independently evolved the body plan later seen in ornithomimosaur dinosaurs a hundred million years later - toothless, long-necked, and built for bipedal locomotion on long cursorial hindlimbs. Its skeleton so closely parallels the structure of Cretaceous ostrich-mimics that the closely related Shuvosaurus was initially classified as a dinosaur before its true pseudosuchian affinities were recognised. A crocodile relative built like an ostrich-mimic: Effigia is entirely toothless, with a probable keratinous beak, a long neck, reduced forelimbs and hindlimbs built for bipedal locomotion. This is the body plan of Cretaceous ornithomimosaurs like Gallimimus and Ornithomimus. The convergence is not superficial - the resemblance was close enough that the related Shuvosaurus was initially classified as a dinosaur rather than a pseudosuchian before Effigia's preparation clarified the picture. Hidden in a museum for half a century: The specimen was collected by Edwin Colbert during the 1947-48 Ghost Ranch excavations alongside hundreds of Coelophysis. Colbert did not believe large vertebrates other than Coelophysis were present in the quarry, and most blocks were never opened. Nesbitt found Effigia in 2004 while searching for additional Coelophysis material. Fifty-six years had passed between collection and identification. The Triassic as an evolutionary experiment: Effigia is one of several Triassic pseudosuchians that independently evolved body plans later associated with dinosaurs. The crocodile line was experimenting with bipedality, toothless beaks and cursorial limb proportions at the same time early dinosaurs were first appearing. The Triassic was not a prelude to dinosaur dominance - it was a period of genuine competition between archosaur lineages testing similar ecological solutions. Dinosaurs were not inevitable: The end-Triassic extinction cleared the pseudosuchians and handed ecological space to dinosaurs - not because dinosaurs had superior anatomy, but because the extinction removed the competition. Effigia shows that the body plans we associate with dinosaurs were available to other lineages . What dinosaurs had, at least in part, was better luck at the end of the Triassic. Jaw biomechanics suggest Effigia was a specialist browser on softer plant material , its weak jaws and probable keratinous beak suited to cropping low vegetation rather than processing tough or resistant food. Beyond this, behaviour cannot be reliably inferred from a single specimen with no associated fauna or trackways. Effigia belongs to Shuvosauridae within Poposauroidea, a diverse clade of pseudosuchian archosaurs that included armoured herbivores, large predators and, in the shuvosaurids, these convergently dinosaur-like bipeds. The end-Triassic extinction eliminated most pseudosuchian diversity, leaving only the crocodyliform lineage to survive. The ecological roles that pseudosuchians held in the Triassic were subsequently taken by the dinosaurs that outlasted them. Effigia demonstrates that the body plans we call distinctively dinosaurian were not exclusive to dinosaurs - and its fifty-six years in an unopened museum jacket is a reminder of what collections may still contain.

title: Effigia — fossil record
text: Single nearly complete skeleton (holotype AMNH FR 30587), American Museum of Natural History, New York; collected 1947-48, described 2006 Evidence level: Good. Whitaker Quarry, Ghost Ranch, Rio Arriba County, New Mexico, USA Chinle Formation (siltstone member) Material: Nearly complete skeleton including skull, Vertebrae, Limb bones. The holotype was collected during Edwin Colbert's 1947-48 Ghost Ranch excavations and stored in an unopened plaster jacket. Sterling Nesbitt identified it in 2004 while studying Coelophysis material from the same quarry. Formally described by Nesbitt and Norell in the Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History (2006). The species name honours painter Georgia O'Keeffe, who lived near Ghost Ranch; the generic name, meaning 'ghost', refers to both the locality and the decades the specimen spent unrecognised.

title: Effigia — Are Effigia and Shuvosaurus the same genus?
text: Are Effigia and Shuvosaurus the same genus? Both are closely related shuvosaurids and some researchers have proposed synonymising them on anatomical grounds. Valid separate genera (moderate confidence): The position maintained by Nesbitt and Norell (2006) and most subsequent analyses. Effigia is a junior synonym of Shuvosaurus (low confidence): Proposed by Lucas and colleagues (2007). Not widely adopted and not accepted by Nesbitt.

title: Nigersaurus — overview
text: A rebbachisaurid sauropod from the Early Cretaceous of the Tenere Desert, Niger, Nigersaurus taqueti carried over 500 replaceable teeth arranged in transverse batteries at the front of a downward-facing muzzle, replacing each tooth as fast as every 14 days as it grazed continuously at ground level - a feeding system convergent with hadrosaurs and ceratopsids, arrived at by an entirely separate lineage. Its skull bones are reduced to thin struts enclosing enormous fenestrae, the least structural material of any large-bodied herbivore skull known, all weight stripped away to support the one function of sweeping a wide muzzle through low vegetation. A skull built for the ground: The muzzle faces directly downward rather than forward, and the inner ear's semicircular canal - revealed by CT scanning - confirms this as a habitual posture, not an accident of preservation. Nigersaurus was grazing at ground level , cropping low ferns and horsetails like a Cretaceous lawnmower rather than reaching into tree canopies like most sauropods. 500 teeth and a 14-day replacement cycle: The dental battery consists of more than 500 teeth arranged in transverse rows at the front of the jaw. Tooth replacement occurred as fast as every 14 days - the fastest known rate among dinosaurs, confirmed by crown thin-section analysis in Sereno et al. (2007). The entire skull was engineered around a single function: eating continuously at low cost on abrasive vegetation. A skull so light it was nearly transparent: The skull bones are reduced to thin struts enclosing huge fenestrae - many bones under 2 millimetres thick - making the structure extraordinarily pneumatic. Sereno described it as having the least structural material of any large-bodied herbivore skull known . Weight reduction allowed the low-held head to be swept side to side across vegetation without muscular strain. Sauropods went sideways: Every other sauropod fed by sweeping the neck vertically or reaching upward. Nigersaurus shows the diplodocoid lineage took a very different branch - specialising downward , evolving a tooth battery convergent with ornithischians to exploit the ground-level niche that most of its relatives ignored entirely. Nigersaurus grazed in low floodplain vegetation, sweeping its wide muzzle through ferns and groundcover in a lateral arc. The speed of tooth replacement implies near-constant feeding on abrasive plant material. Unlike the large diplodocoids of the Jurassic, it was not a high browser - its neck proportions and skull orientation make that mechanically implausible. It shared its environment with the spinosaurid Suchomimus and the abelisaurid Kryptops, both potential predators, though direct predation evidence on Nigersaurus is not known. Rebbachisauridae were a Cretaceous lineage of diplodocoid sauropods, smaller and differently specialised than the giants of the Jurassic. Nigersaurus represents the extreme end of rebbachisaurid dental specialisation - an evolutionary endpoint rather than a transitional form. The dental battery is a convergent solution to the hadrosaur and ceratopsid batteries, arrived at by a completely separate lineage across hundreds of millions of years of separation. Nigersaurus shows that sauropod body plans were far more varied than the familiar long-neck-high-browser image suggests - and that a dental battery convergent with hadrosaurs evolved independently in a lineage separated from them by over 150 million years of evolution.

title: Nigersaurus — fossil record
text: Multiple partial skulls and postcranial material from the Elrhaz Formation, Gadoufaoua, Niger; holotype MNN GAD512 Evidence level: Good. Gadoufaoua, Tenere Desert, Agadez Region, Niger Elrhaz Formation Material: Partial skulls, Vertebrae, Limb bones, Dental battery material. First fossils collected during French expeditions of 1965-72 led by Philippe Taquet, briefly described in 1976. Formally named Nigersaurus taqueti by Sereno and colleagues in 1999 from material recovered in 1997 expeditions. A fuller skeletal description and CT analysis were published in PLOS ONE (Sereno, Wilson, Witmer, Whitlock, Maga, Ide and Rowe, 2007, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0001230), establishing inner ear posture evidence and tooth replacement rate. The species name honours Philippe Taquet.

title: Nigersaurus — Did Nigersaurus habitually hold its head pointing downward?
text: Did Nigersaurus habitually hold its head pointing downward? Head posture in extinct animals is often inferred from neck structure alone; Nigersaurus has direct inner ear evidence from CT scanning. Habitual downward-facing posture (strong confidence): The lateral semicircular canal of the inner ear indicates the head was held with the muzzle pointing toward the ground in the alert, resting position. Confirmed by CT analysis in Sereno et al. (2007, PLOS ONE, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0001230). Flexible posture as in other sauropods (low confidence): Some researchers argue neck posture in sauropods was more variable than inner ear evidence suggests, but the Nigersaurus skull design independently supports ground-level feeding regardless of posture flexibility.

title: Gastonia — overview
text: A polacanthid ankylosaur from the Early Cretaceous of Utah, Gastonia burgei was a medium-sized armoured herbivore around 5 to 6 metres long, carrying a fused sacral shield, large triangular shoulder spines and a tail armed with horizontal osteoderms rather than the club seen in derived ankylosaurids. More complete material exists for Gastonia than for any other polacanthid, making it the anatomical reference against which less well-preserved members of the group are reconstructed - including the type specimen found in the same quarry as Utahraptor, the largest known dromaeosaurid. The best-known polacanthid: Hundreds of disarticulated bones have been recovered from the Gaston Quarry and the nearby Dalton Wells Quarry, representing multiple individuals. This abundance makes Gastonia the reference species for polacanthid anatomy - reconstructions of the British Polacanthus, known from far less material, rely heavily on Gastonia comparisons to fill in missing elements. Armour with a geometry problem: The disarticulated nature of the bonebed material means the precise arrangement of Gastonia's many osteoderms and spines is uncertain. It is difficult to determine exactly how many spikes a given individual carried or how they were arranged along the body - the bones are present in abundance, but assigning them confidently to specific positions requires care that not all reconstructions exercise. Found with Utahraptor: The primary bonebed produced material of both Gastonia and Utahraptor ostrommaysorum - the largest known dromaeosaurid, reaching up to 7 metres. The co-occurrence places the most heavily armed polacanthid and the largest raptor yet found in the same deposit , though direct predation evidence is not documented from the site. A second species from a monospecific bonebed: A 2016 redescription described a second species, G. lorriemcwhinneyae, from the Ruby Ranch Member - a stratigraphically higher unit - based on a monospecific bonebed interpreted as a mass mortality event. Two species, two independent bonebed events, two different preservation contexts within the same formation. Gastonia was a low browser in a partly wooded, seasonally dry environment with riverine forest separated by open ground. Its sacral shield and extensive lateral armour would have made it difficult to attack from above or the sides, and the shoulder spines were oriented to protect the flanks specifically. Whether the tail was used actively in defence is inferred from osteoderm arrangement but not directly evidenced. Polacanthids were a group of early-diverging ankylosaurs most abundant in the Early Cretaceous of Europe and North America. They retained a more primitive armour arrangement than the derived ankylosaurids, lacking the tail club that characterises that group. Gastonia's close relationship to the British Polacanthus suggests a faunal connection between Early Cretaceous North America and Europe, though polacanthid phylogeny remains contested across analyses. Gastonia is the anatomical anchor for polacanthid ankylosaurs - the species that makes reconstructing others possible - and its shared quarry with Utahraptor is one of the few direct co-occurrences of a heavily armoured prey animal and a large predator in the Early Cretaceous record.

title: Gastonia — fossil record
text: Hundreds of disarticulated bones from the Gaston Quarry and Dalton Wells Quarry, Grand County, Utah; holotype CEUM 1307 (skull) Evidence level: Rich. Gaston Quarry and Dalton Wells Quarry, Grand County, Utah, USA Cedar Mountain Formation (Yellow Cat Member; Ruby Ranch Member for G. lorriemcwhinneyae) Material: Multiple skulls, Vertebrae, Limb elements, Extensive osteoderm and spine material, Sacral shield. The primary bonebed was discovered by paleoartist Robert Gaston in 1989; the genus is named for him. Formally described by James Kirkland in 1998. The holotype skull is housed at the USU Eastern Prehistoric Museum. A detailed redescription of G. burgei and description of the second species G. lorriemcwhinneyae was published in Neues Jahrbuch fur Geologie und Palaontologie (Kinneer, Carpenter and Shaw, 2016). Utahraptor ostrommaysorum material was recovered from the same primary quarry level, making this one of the most ecologically evocative Early Cretaceous sites in North America.

title: Gastonia — Is Gastonia a polacanthid within a valid Polacanthidae, or a basal nodosaurid?
text: Is Gastonia a polacanthid within a valid Polacanthidae, or a basal nodosaurid? Polacanthid phylogeny has been unstable across analyses, with different studies placing members within or outside Nodosauridae. Polacanthidae as a valid early-branching ankylosaur clade (moderate confidence): The arrangement followed by Kirkland (1998) and the 2016 redescription; polacanthids as distinct from nodosaurids. Polacanthids as basal nodosaurids (moderate confidence): Some phylogenetic analyses nest Gastonia and relatives within Nodosauridae rather than as a separate clade. The question is not settled and may depend on character selection.

title: Abelisaurus — overview
text: An abelisaurid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia, Abelisaurus comahuensis is known from a single partial skull - yet that skull was distinctive enough for Bonaparte and Novas to establish a new family, Abelisauridae, and predict a Gondwanan distribution for the group at a time when almost nothing was known of southern hemisphere large theropods. Everything about the animal below the neck is inferred from better-preserved relatives described in the four decades since. One skull, one family: Bonaparte and Novas recognised that the skull's proportions - high cranium, huge antorbital fenestra, wide lower temporal fenestra, near-closed orbits - were sufficiently different from all known theropod families to warrant a new one. A single damaged skull created the taxonomic framework for understanding Gondwanan large predators across four continents. What a skull tells you and what it doesn't: Because only the skull is known, almost everything about Abelisaurus's body is inferred from better-preserved relatives. Its approximate size comes from skull-to-body scaling using Carnotaurus and Majungasaurus as references. Its forelimb proportions, tail shape, skin texture and locomotion are entirely unknown from direct evidence - yet it has been reconstructed with confidence in popular media for decades. The southern answer to tyrannosaurs: Before abelisaurids were understood, some fragmentary Gondwanan theropod material had been tentatively attributed to tyrannosaurs, implying a cosmopolitan distribution for that group. Abelisaurus and subsequent abelisaurid finds showed that the southern hemisphere had its own independent lineage of large apex predators - one that convergently evolved some similar ecological roles through a completely different evolutionary pathway. Uncertain formation, certain importance: The holotype was initially described as coming from the Allen Formation but subsequent analysis indicated the Anacleto Formation, an older unit. This stratigraphic uncertainty has not been fully resolved in the literature - a reminder that even the basic contextual data for historically significant specimens can remain contested decades after description. Behaviour cannot be directly inferred from a partial skull. Based on well-preserved relatives, abelisaurids were bipedal predators with relatively short and reduced forelimbs, deep skulls adapted for strong biting, and robust hindlimbs. Abelisaurus's skull proportions are consistent with this general model, but specific claims about its ecology go beyond what the single specimen supports. Abelisaurus is the namesake of Abelisauridae and Abelisauroidea, the dominant large theropod lineage of the Cretaceous Gondwana. The group is now known from Argentina, Brazil, Madagascar, India, France and North Africa, confirming Bonaparte and Novas's original suggestion of Gondwanan distribution. Where Abelisaurus itself sits within the family - whether as a basal abelisaurid or deeper within the tree - is not settled from the available skull material alone. Abelisaurus founded a family from a single skull - and that family turned out to be the answer to a long-standing puzzle about why Gondwanan Cretaceous predator faunas looked so different from those of the north.

title: Abelisaurus — fossil record
text: Single partial skull (holotype MC 11098), Cipolletti Museum, Rio Negro Province, Argentina Evidence level: Fragment. Lago Pellegrini area, Rio Negro Province, Patagonia, Argentina Anacleto Formation, Neuquen Group (formation attribution remains debated; originally described as Allen Formation) Material: Partial skull approximately 85 cm long, missing right maxilla, jugal, quadratojugal, squamosal and most of palate. Discovered by Roberto Abel, then director of the Cipolletti Museum, and described by Bonaparte and Novas in Ameghiniana (1985) - the paper that established Abelisauridae. All postcranial anatomy is inferred from subsequently described abelisaurids, particularly Carnotaurus (also 1985) and Majungasaurus. The formation attribution has been debated, with some sources placing the holotype in the Allen Formation and others in the Anacleto Formation, affecting its precise stratigraphic age.

title: Abelisaurus — Where does Abelisaurus sit within Abelisauridae?
text: Where does Abelisaurus sit within Abelisauridae? The skull material is insufficient to resolve fine-grained phylogenetic questions within the family, and the formation attribution affects its stratigraphic context. Basal abelisaurid, outside Carnotaurinae (moderate confidence): The position in most analyses based on skull characters. Consistent with its early description and geographic context. Possible synonym of Aucasaurus (low confidence): Suggested by some researchers on the basis of comparable morphology; not widely accepted and not resolvable without more complete material for one or both taxa.

title: Nyasasaurus — overview
text: A dinosauriform archosaur from the Middle Triassic of Tanzania, Nyasasaurus parringtoni is either the oldest known dinosaur or the closest known relative of the group - the humerus and vertebrae that constitute its entire fossil record cannot resolve which. If it is a dinosaur, it predates the next oldest unambiguous members of the group from Argentina by approximately 10 to 15 million years, and its bone histology shows the rapid growth rates characteristic of early dinosaurs rather than the slower growth of most contemporary archosaurs. Dinosaur or dinosaur's closest relative - the material cannot tell us: Nyasasaurus possesses a combination of features consistent with early dinosaurs - an elongated deltopectoral crest on the humerus, at least three sacral vertebrae, and rapidly deposited woven bone tissue - but the known material is too incomplete to place it unambiguously inside or outside Dinosauria . Nesbitt et al.'s own phrasing was 'either the earliest known member of, or the sister-taxon to, Dinosauria'. Both remain live possibilities. Bone histology pushes the claim: The humerus preserves highly vascularised, rapidly deposited woven bone tissue with no lines of arrested growth - a growth pattern consistent with early dinosaurs and distinct from the slower growth of most contemporary archosaurs . This physiological signal is part of what makes the dinosaur placement plausible even from such limited material. A southern Pangaean origin for dinosaurs: Nyasasaurus comes from Tanzania, adding African evidence to the argument that dinosaurs originated in southern Pangaea - the same general region as the earliest unambiguous Argentinian dinosaurs. Its description reinforces a picture of a Gondwanan cradle for the group in the Middle Triassic. Collected in the 1930s, described in 2013: The specimens were collected during F. Rex Parrington's Cambridge expeditions to Tanzania in the 1930s. Alan Charig informally assigned the genus name in his 1956 doctoral dissertation but died before publishing a formal description. The gap between collection and description was approximately 75 years - another example of significant scientific value locked in museum collections awaiting reassessment. Diet, behaviour and ecology are unknown. No skull or teeth are preserved, removing the primary evidence for feeding ecology. Size is estimated from vertebral dimensions as roughly 2-3 metres but this is a rough approximation. No inferences about feeding or social behaviour are warranted from the available fossils. If Nyasasaurus is a true dinosaur, it extends the minimum age of the group to the Anisian stage of the Middle Triassic at approximately 243 Ma, implying a much longer ghost lineage for the earliest dinosaur lineages than the Argentinian record alone suggests. If it is the sister taxon to Dinosauria, it still demonstrates the dinosaur stem was physiologically active and growing rapidly in the Middle Triassic. Either outcome places dinosaur origins earlier and further south than previously confirmed. Nyasasaurus sits at the most contested point in dinosaur evolutionary history - the moment the lineage first appeared - and shows that even with the best available evidence, that moment cannot yet be precisely located in time or phylogeny.

title: Nyasasaurus — fossil record
text: Holotype NHMUK R6856 (humerus and partial vertebral column), Natural History Museum, London; referred specimen SAM-PK-K10654 (four vertebrae), Iziko South African Museum, Cape Town Evidence level: Fragment. Lifua Member, Manda Beds, Ruhuhu Basin, Tanzania Manda Beds (Lifua Member) Material: Humerus, Partial vertebral column, Four additional vertebrae (referred specimen). Specimens collected by F.
