Hesperornis and Ichthyornis, described in 1872–75, showed birds with fully reptilian teeth - a transitional stage that Thomas Huxley immediately identified as decisive evidence for the evolutionary connection between birds and reptiles. Darwin reportedly called the discovery the best support of the theory of evolution since the publication of the Origin. Marsh himself was cautious about evolutionary interpretation, but the specimens he placed in collections forced others to draw the conclusion. The Bone Wars tactics were genuinely destructive to science as well as to Marsh and Cope personally. Both men published species names from inadequate material to establish priority, resulting in a tangle of synonyms and poorly described genera that took decades to sort out. Marsh's crew destroyed remaining specimens at Como Bluff rather than let Cope's team access them. He used his position on the U.S. Geological Survey to cut off Cope's government funding. And the famous 'Brontosaurus' error - applying a name to the wrong head - persisted in museum displays well into the late 20th century. The name was eventually rehabilitated in 2015 as a valid genus, but the episode illustrates how competitive pressure distorted careful science. Stegosaurus, Triceratops, and Diplodocus - three of the most visited entries in DinoDex - were all Marsh's names. The collections he built at Yale and the Smithsonian remain active research resources. But DinoDex's "Still Under Study" sections, which flag contested species identifications and incomplete material, exist partly because of the legacy of hasty naming that the Bone Wars made normal.

title: E.D. Cope
text: The more brilliant of the two rivals - and the one who lost. Described 56 new dinosaur species, over 1,400 scientific papers across vertebrate palaeontology and herpetology, and the theoretical framework of 'Cope's Rule' on size increase in evolution - while waging an escalating rivalry against Marsh that consumed both men's fortunes. Edward Drinker Cope was born in 1840 in Philadelphia into a prosperous Quaker family, which provided both the money and the moral self-certainty he would need for the Bone Wars. He was a prodigy - publishing his first scientific paper at 19, and accumulating comparative anatomical expertise across reptiles, amphibians, fish, and mammals simultaneously. He had no university degree in palaeontology; his depth came from voracious self-teaching and an extraordinary memory. He was impulsive, emotionally volatile, and capable of both brilliant synthesis and careless error. By the end of his life he had spent his inherited wealth, was reduced to selling parts of his fossil collection to fund new fieldwork, and was living in his Philadelphia house surrounded by specimens. He died in 1897, aged 56. Cope described Camarasaurus, Coelophysis (from Ghost Ranch, though later fieldwork clarified the discovery), Monoclonius, Dimetrodon (technically a synapsid, not a dinosaur), and dozens of other species across the vertebrate tree. He was fast in the field and had a better intuitive grasp of comparative anatomy than most contemporaries - his errors, like the Elasmosaurus head inversion, came from haste rather than ignorance. Cope contributed 'Cope's Rule' - the empirical observation that animal lineages tend toward larger body sizes over evolutionary time - a concept that has been debated, refined, and periodically contested ever since. More significantly for palaeontology, his comparative anatomical work, conducted across a vast range of taxa, demonstrated that the vertebrate body plan showed evolutionary continuity. He was an open Darwinian in a way Marsh was not, and his theoretical ambitions were broader, if his political skills were weaker. The Elasmosaurus incident of 1869 - Cope placed the head of this long-necked marine reptile at the wrong end, was corrected by Marsh, who then told others about it - launched a feud that consumed them both financially. Cope's 1892 broadside in the New York Herald, accusing Marsh of plagiarism, theft, and incompetence, prompted an equally brutal response. Both men were partly right about the other's misconduct. Cope's collections were also built partly on the work of paid field agents whose contributions were rarely acknowledged. Cope's story is the more interesting personality study of the Bone Wars pair. His willingness to theorise beyond his evidence - sometimes disastrously - but also to write across the whole of vertebrate evolution connects to DinoDex's interest in what the fossils reveal versus what science is still arguing about. His ghost haunts every badly named species that later got cleaned up.

title: Barnum Brown
text: He found the two most important T. rex skeletons ever excavated, wore a fur coat in the field, and reportedly worked for American intelligence. Discovered the first documented Tyrannosaurus rex remains (1902) and built the American Museum of Natural History's dinosaur collection into the finest in the world over a 66-year career. Barnum Brown was born in 1873 in Carbondale, Kansas, and named - against all odds - after P.T. Barnum. He grew up collecting fossil shells from ploughed fields and studied under vertebrate palaeontologist Samuel Williston before joining the American Museum of Natural History in 1897, where he would remain, in various capacities, until 1963. He was known for wearing a fur coat in the field, for charm that got him access to private lands across four continents, and - according to a documented account in the American Museum's records - for occasionally supplying information to American agencies during his overseas expeditions. He never completed his doctoral thesis. He wrote almost no scientific papers. He found more significant fossil material than nearly anyone before or since. In the summer of 1902, digging in Hell Creek, Montana, Brown found the first documented partial skeleton of Tyrannosaurus rex. Six years later he found the better-preserved specimen that became the centrepiece of the AMNH's dinosaur hall. He also collected major specimens of Ankylosaurus (which he named), Corythosaurus, and many others from the Red Deer River in Alberta - using a flat-bottomed barge as a field camp, arguably the most elegant logistical solution in the history of fossil collecting. Brown's contribution was less conceptual than infrastructural. By building the AMNH collection into a global resource, he created the physical basis for twentieth-century vertebrate palaeontology. The specimens he collected have been re-studied repeatedly as new methods became available: bone histology on his Hell Creek T. rex material, CT scanning on specimens he brought back from India and Burma. He showed that a dedicated field collector, working systematically over decades, could transform what a science knows simply by finding more of it. Brown received almost none of the scientific credit for T. rex during his lifetime. Henry Fairfield Osborn, the AMNH president who formally described and named the animal, was credited in contemporary accounts as the discoverer - a misrepresentation that Brown reportedly accepted without visible protest. He collected from multiple countries under conditions that would now be considered problematic - acquiring specimens without formal agreements, exploiting local knowledge without crediting or compensating its sources. Tyrannosaurus rex in DinoDex carries the full weight of what Brown found - the most studied predator in the fossil record, whose growth rates, bite force, and senses are known in remarkable detail. That depth of knowledge is possible because Brown found two good skeletons, and the AMNH kept them and made them available. The gap between Brown's field genius and his public recognition is also quietly embedded in DinoDex's philosophy: the fossils tell the story; the names attached to their discovery are often an accident of institutional power.

title: Roy Chapman Andrews
text: His team found the first dinosaur eggs - though they assigned them to the wrong dinosaur for seventy years. Led the American Museum of Natural History's Central Asiatic Expeditions (1922–1930) to the Gobi Desert, recovering the first scientifically recognised dinosaur eggs, Protoceratops, Velociraptor, and Oviraptor. Roy Chapman Andrews started his career at the AMNH sweeping floors and worked his way into fieldwork through determination and self-promotion. He was an explorer and populariser as much as a technical scientist - his books for younger readers are credited with inspiring a cohort of palaeontologists who went on to work in the Gobi themselves from the 1990s onwards. He became Director of the AMNH in 1934, found administrative life suffocating compared to fieldwork, and retired in 1941. The claim that Indiana Jones was modelled on Andrews has circulated widely; it remains unconfirmed by George Lucas, though Andrews' lifestyle - expeditions, sandstorms, pistol, remote landscapes - supplied obvious visual material. The 1922–1930 Central Asiatic Expeditions recovered Protoceratops andrewsi (named for him), Velociraptor mongoliensis, the Oviraptor specimen found apparently brooding (later reinterpreted), and - most famously - the first fossilised dinosaur eggs ever recognised by science, found at the Flaming Cliffs (Shabarakh Usu) in 1923. The eggs were initially attributed to Protoceratops, the most abundant dinosaur at the site. When later work showed they actually belonged to Oviraptor - found on top of them, which had been assumed to indicate nest-raiding - it corrected a famous misconception and rehabilitated the 'egg thief' as a devoted parent. Before 1923, the reproductive biology of dinosaurs was a complete blank. No fossilised dinosaur egg had been scientifically confirmed. The Flaming Cliffs eggs proved that dinosaurs were oviparous - that they laid eggs and apparently nested - giving palaeontology its first physical evidence of dinosaur life history beyond bones. The subsequent re-attribution of the eggs to Oviraptor (confirmed in the 1990s) added a second idea: that at least some dinosaurs brooded their eggs in a bird-like posture, reinforcing the dinosaur-bird behavioural connection even before the wave of feathered fossils from China. The Central Asiatic Expeditions operated with the logic of empire. The AMNH spent enormous sums extracting biological and geological material from Mongolia during a period of Chinese and Mongolian political transition. The ostensible goal - to find early human ancestors - was never achieved. Mongolian and Chinese authorities eventually restricted further work, and the collections from the expeditions remain in New York rather than in the countries of origin, a matter of ongoing sensitivity. Andrews was also a prolific hunter whose field journals record extensive killing of wildlife. His outsized celebrity relative to the actual palaeontologists on the expedition (Walter Granger was the chief scientist) is another credit imbalance worth noting. Protoceratops, Velociraptor, and Oviraptor in DinoDex all trace their discoverers to the Central Asiatic Expeditions. The Oviraptor entry specifically describes the egg-attribution error and its correction - precisely the kind of science-in-revision story that makes Andrews' expedition scientifically rich rather than just adventurous.

title: John Ostrom
text: One fossil found in Montana changed everything we thought we knew about dinosaurs. Described Deinonychus antirrhopus in 1969 and argued it was active, warm-blooded, and bird-like - launching the Dinosaur Renaissance and reviving the century-old hypothesis that birds descended from dinosaurs. John Ostrom was born in New York in 1928 and intended to study medicine before a correspondence with palaeontologist George Gaylord Simpson redirected him. He completed his doctorate at Columbia and spent most of his career at Yale's Peabody Museum of Natural History, where he discovered Deinonychus during fieldwork in Montana in 1964. He was a careful, methodical scientist who described his conclusions with deliberate understatement, and then watched as Robert Bakker took that understatement and turned it into a movement. He died in Litchfield, Connecticut, in 2005. In 1964, Ostrom's field team found a sickle claw in the hills of Montana's Cloverly Formation that led to a two-season excavation and over a thousand bones from at least four individuals of a new species. His 1969 monograph on Deinonychus antirrhopus argued in detail that the animal was bipedal, fast, and equipped for active predation in a way incompatible with the cold-blooded, sluggish dinosaur of textbooks. In 1970, while examining a museum specimen in the Netherlands, he recognised a supposed pterosaur as an Archaeopteryx - and then spent years working out the anatomical evidence that birds were directly descended from theropod dinosaurs. Before Ostrom, the consensus view - established since the early 20th century and entrenched by Henry Fairfield Osborn's authority - was that dinosaurs were evolutionary failures: cold-blooded, slow, superseded by mammals. Ostrom's Deinonychus paper demolished this in a single publication. The anatomical detail he provided - limb proportions, tail structure, claw geometry, upright posture - made an active, warm physiology not just possible but necessary. His subsequent papers on Archaeopteryx and the theropod ancestry of birds provided the anatomical framework that the Chinese feathered fossils of the 1990s confirmed. He didn't just describe a new dinosaur; he changed the questions the field asked. The popular association of Deinonychus with the 'Velociraptors' of Jurassic Park - via Greg Paul's 1988 reclassification and Crichton's novel - created a public image of Ostrom's discovery that bore little relationship to the actual animal or the actual science. Ostrom was reportedly ambivalent about the cultural impact: pleased that palaeontology reached mass audiences, less pleased that the science was badly mangled in the process. It is also worth noting that Bakker, not Ostrom, coined the phrase 'dinosaur renaissance' and drove much of the public momentum - Ostrom's contribution is sometimes underrated relative to his more publicly visible student. Deinonychus, Velociraptor, and Archaeopteryx - three of the most analytically rich entries in DinoDex - all carry, at their core, the ideas that Ostrom developed between 1964 and 1976. The section in the DinoDex entry for Deinonychus on 'raptor prey restraint' (grip, not slash) is itself a correction of the Jurassic Park image, which itself misread Ostrom's work. DinoDex's commitment to separating what fossils show from what is inferred is, in spirit, exactly the programme Ostrom followed.

title: Robert Bakker
text: He was an undergraduate when he drew a running Deinonychus that changed how people imagined dinosaurs. He has been arguing ever since. Coined the term 'dinosaur renaissance'; argued publicly and persistently for warm-blooded, active dinosaurs; authored The Dinosaur Heresies (1986); and made the new view of dinosaurs legible to the broadest possible public. Robert Bakker was born in New Jersey in 1945 and arrived at Yale as an undergraduate at precisely the moment John Ostrom was excavating Deinonychus. He drew the frontispiece for Ostrom's 1969 monograph - a Deinonychus running on one toe with its sickle claw raised, utterly unlike any previous dinosaur illustration. He went on to a PhD at Harvard and a career that has run partly in academia, partly in museums (he has long been affiliated with the Houston Museum of Natural Science), and substantially in public science. He is tall, bearded, wears a cowboy hat, and has been a reliable media presence in palaeontology since the 1970s. He was a consultant on Jurassic Park. Bakker's primary contributions are theoretical and communicative rather than specimen-based. His 1968 undergraduate paper 'The Superiority of Dinosaurs' was the first published salvo of the renaissance. His 1975 Scientific American article 'Dinosaur Renaissance' named the movement and presented a systematic case - bone structure, predator-prey ratios, posture - for warm-blooded dinosaurs. His 1986 book The Dinosaur Heresies made all of this accessible to a general readership with his own distinctive illustrations. He also conducted fieldwork in Wyoming's Como Bluff and described several genera, including evidence that has been cited in discussions of Allosaurus nesting behaviour. Where Ostrom provided the anatomical evidence, Bakker provided the argument and the image. His illustrations of running, athletic dinosaurs - appearing in Scientific American, in his books, and in popular culture - replaced earlier swamp-dwelling reconstructions in the public imagination before any scientific consensus had been reached. This is both his achievement and his methodological limitation: he argued with great confidence for positions that sometimes outran the evidence available at the time. Some of these positions have since been partially vindicated; others have not. Bakker's style - adversarial, confident, performatively contrarian - served the renaissance well when there was a genuine consensus to challenge. As he grew older and the warm-blooded-dinosaur position became mainstream, his continued heterodoxy sometimes shaded into contrarianism for its own sake. His rejection of the asteroid impact as the primary cause of the K-Pg extinction has been increasingly difficult to maintain against the accumulating evidence. In 2017 he was briefly suspended from the Houston Museum of Natural Science following allegations of professional misconduct, though he was later reinstated; the museum released limited information about the circumstances. The DinoDex aesthetic - active animals, confident claims about behaviour where evidence supports them, genuine uncertainty flagged clearly - is in direct dialogue with the tradition Bakker represents. His influence is embedded in how the site's entries read: not as dry taxonomy but as animal stories. His errors are equally instructive, and DinoDex's 'Still Under Study' sections exist to prevent exactly the kind of overclaiming that Bakker sometimes modelled.

title: Luis Alvarez & Walter Alvarez
text: They came from outside palaeontology and found the answer everyone else had missed. Identified the iridium anomaly at the Cretaceous-Palaeogene boundary in 1980, providing the first physical evidence for a major asteroid impact at the end of the Cretaceous - and igniting the debate that eventually established the impact hypothesis for the mass extinction. Luis Walter Alvarez was a Nobel Prize-winning physicist at Berkeley, best known before 1977 for his work in particle physics. His son Walter was a geologist working on Cretaceous-Palaeogene boundary sediments in the Apennines near Gubbio, Italy, trying to use the magnetic reversal record to calculate sedimentation rates. The thin clay layer at the K-Pg boundary blocked his calculations: it appeared to have been deposited almost instantaneously, relative to geological time. He asked his father whether nuclear physics could help date the layer. Luis suggested measuring iridium - a platinum-group element rare on Earth's surface but common in meteorites - as a measure of accumulation rate. The iridium concentration was not merely slightly elevated: it was many times higher than background levels at Gubbio, and the same anomaly was subsequently found at boundary sites on multiple continents. The 1980 paper by Luis Alvarez, Walter Alvarez, Frank Asaro, and Helen Michel in Science reported iridium anomalies at Gubbio, a Danish site, and a New Zealand site simultaneously. The team proposed that the iridium came from a large asteroid or comet, and that the impact released enough energy to trigger the global extinction that closed the Cretaceous. The hypothesis predicted a crater of around 150–200 km diameter. That crater - Chicxulub, off the Yucatán coast - was identified in 1991 from geophysical data and confirmed by core drilling. The iridium layer, the crater, and shocked quartz minerals from the impact now constitute three independent lines of physical evidence for the same event. Before the Alvarez hypothesis, the consensus explanation for the K-Pg extinction was gradual climate change - sea level regression, volcanic activity, gradual cooling - over millions of years. The idea of a catastrophic, rapid, globally simultaneous extinction by an extraterrestrial impact was not seriously entertained by most geologists or palaeontologists. The Alvarez paper changed the framing of extinction science irreversibly: it introduced the concept of punctuated catastrophe rather than gradual change, and it made the search for physical evidence of past extinctions - rather than pattern-reading from fossil gaps alone - the new standard. Luis Alvarez was a physicist, not a palaeontologist, and his initial dismissiveness toward palaeontologists - including reported comments suggesting they were not rigorous scientists - caused lasting resentment in the field. The impact hypothesis was fiercely resisted for most of the 1980s, partly on scientific grounds (the crater had not yet been identified) and partly because palaeontologists felt their discipline was being invaded by outsiders who did not understand the fossil record's limitations. The question of whether volcanic eruptions from the Deccan Traps also contributed to the extinction, and whether different groups of organisms went extinct at exactly the same horizon, is still an active area of research. The K-Pg extinction is the frame within which every DinoDex entry ends. The statement that non-avian dinosaurs went extinct 66 million years ago is the direct consequence of the Alvarez discovery: we now know not just that they went extinct, but when, how fast, and why. Triceratops and Mosasaurus in DinoDex are the last survivors before that boundary - their absence from the geological record above it is part of the iridium-layer evidence.

title: Xu Xing
text: He has named more feathered dinosaurs than any other scientist in history. Described over 80 new dinosaur species from China, including the four-winged Microraptor, the long-armed Yi qi, and dozens of feathered theropods that transformed understanding of the dinosaur-bird transition. Xu Xing was born in 1969 in Xinjiang and studied at Peking University before joining the Institute of Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoanthropology (IVPP) in Beijing, where he has spent his career. He came to palaeontology through formal academic training rather than early collecting. He began publishing in the 1990s and has maintained a rate of species description that has no parallel in the modern history of vertebrate palaeontology: he has named or co-named over 80 dinosaur taxa, more than any other living scientist. He is simultaneously a field collector, a descriptive anatomist, and a phylogenetic analyst who works within an international network of collaborators spanning China, the United States, Europe, and elsewhere. Xu's most consequential descriptions include Microraptor gui in 2003 - the four-winged theropod that demonstrated gliding as a plausible early step in the evolution of flight - and Anchiornis huxleyi in 2009, a feathered dinosaur older than Archaeopteryx that extended the known bird lineage deeper into the Jurassic. He also described Yi qi in 2015, a bat-winged scansoriopterygid whose patagial flight membrane represented a completely independent experiment in dinosaur flight. His work on Sinornithosaurus, Linheraptor, and dozens of others has steadily filled the anatomical gap between non-avian theropod dinosaurs and modern birds. Xu's work, combined with the Chinese feathered-fossil record more broadly, confirmed and elaborated the hypothesis that John Ostrom had proposed in the 1970s: birds are living dinosaurs, descended from maniraptoran theropods. More specifically, Xu's descriptions showed that the key bird features - feathers, wishbone, forward-facing eyes, air sacs, brooding behaviour - evolved incrementally across many millions of years of theropod evolution, not all at once. Microraptor showed that intermediate stages between non-flying theropods and flying birds were genuinely intermediate in anatomy and behaviour. The shift Xu's work enabled was from 'birds may be related to dinosaurs' to 'birds are living maniraptoran theropods, and the distinction is a matter of degree, not kind.' The Chinese fossil trade presents genuine scientific and legal complications. A significant proportion of feathered fossils from Liaoning have entered international collections through informal or illegal channels, creating problems of provenance and context loss. The Archaeoraptor scandal of 1999 - a faked chimera of two real species sold to National Geographic - was the most damaging example. Xu himself identified the forgery and publicly documented it, an act of scientific integrity that cost him support from some quarters. The broader issue of Chinese fossil law, export controls, and the international market in specimens is an ongoing complication for the field that Xu has navigated with care. Microraptor, Anchiornis, and Yi qi in DinoDex are all Xu's descriptions. The entries specifically address the question of how flight evolved - the trees-down versus ground-up debate, the gliding intermediary hypothesis, the bat-wing experiment - which is precisely the landscape that Xu's work mapped. DinoDex's approach of treating uncertainty explicitly rather than hiding it behind confident reconstruction is particularly appropriate for these entries, where the morphological evidence is rich but the biomechanical interpretation is genuinely contested.

title: José Bonaparte
text: He remade the map of dinosaur evolution by showing that South America was a world apart. Named Carnotaurus, Abelisaurus, Amargasaurus, Mussaurus, and dozens of other South American dinosaurs - demonstrating that Gondwanan continents harboured entirely separate evolutionary lineages from the Northern Hemisphere genera that dominated museum collections. José Fernando Bonaparte was born in Buenos Aires in 1928 and largely taught himself palaeontology through self-education and fieldwork over decades when Argentina had almost no academic palaeontology infrastructure. He worked at the Miguel Lillo Foundation in Tucumán and later at the Museo Argentino de Ciencias Naturales in Buenos Aires. He conducted fieldwork across Patagonia, the Andes, and Brazil from the 1960s through the 1990s, discovering fossil beds that were essentially unknown to international science. He trained the next generation of Argentine palaeontologists - Fernando Novas, Rodolfo Coria, and others either worked with him directly or were inspired by his example - and effectively established Argentine vertebrate palaeontology as a functioning discipline. He died in 2020. Bonaparte named Carnotaurus sastrei in 1985 - one of the most anatomically distinct large theropods ever found, with bull-like horns, a deep skull, and dramatically reduced forelimbs - from Patagonia. He described Abelisaurus, Noasaurus, Amargasaurus, and Mussaurus, the last of which (a sauropodomorph from Triassic Argentina) he first described from hatchlings so tiny they were initially mistaken for embryos. He also contributed to the discovery of early dinosaur material from the Ischigualasto Formation that would later yield Eoraptor and Herrerasaurus to international teams. Over decades he demonstrated that South American dinosaurs were not simply southern extensions of Northern Hemisphere lineages but represented separately evolved groups with their own anatomical signatures. Before Bonaparte, palaeontology was effectively a Northern Hemisphere enterprise. The dinosaurs that stocked museums and textbooks - Tyrannosaurus, Stegosaurus, Diplodocus - came overwhelmingly from North America and Europe, and the implicit assumption was that these were broadly representative of dinosaur evolution. Bonaparte's decades of work demonstrated that Gondwana - the southern supercontinent - carried its own radiation of dinosaurs, the abelisaurids and titanosaurs and early sauropodomorphs, that were not just southern versions of northern forms but genuinely distinct evolutionary lineages shaped by geographic isolation. This geographical reframing of palaeontology - from a Northern Hemisphere discipline to a global science - continues with every new find from Africa, Madagascar, India, and Antarctica. Bonaparte's career unfolded under political conditions that science rarely acknowledges directly: he did his most significant work during and in the aftermath of Argentina's military dictatorship of 1976–1983, in a country where academic institutions were severely disrupted and international collaboration was complicated by limited resources and political instability. The global reach his finds eventually achieved came partly because he was willing to work with international collaborators, and partly because Argentine geology had been largely overlooked by the English-speaking scientific establishment for so long that his finds were genuinely novel to the international community. His limited access to the major journals and conferences that European and North American scientists took for granted meant his work took longer to fully register in the literature than it deserved. Carnotaurus in DinoDex carries the full weight of what Southern Hemisphere palaeontology discovered: a predator so anatomically extreme - horns, vestigial arms, deep skull, racing-body proportions - that it forced a fundamental revision of what abelisaurid theropods could do and be. Argentinosaurus, the largest land animal in the DinoDex, emerged from the Patagonian beds that Bonaparte was among the first to systematically investigate. Together, they stand for the global nature of the story DinoDex tells - a story that would have remained half-told if palaeontology had stayed in the Northern Hemisphere.

title: A world still alive
text: Before the impact Late Cretaceous · just before 66 Ma The Late Cretaceous world was not primitive. It was a fully developed biosphere, rich in diversity and tightly networked. Flowering plants had transformed terrestrial ecosystems over the preceding thirty million years, producing forests, meadows, and flowering understories that did not exist when the dinosaurs first arose. Non-avian dinosaurs were abundant and varied: large ceratopsians like Triceratops , hadrosaurs like Edmontosaurus , ankylosaurs, pachycephalosaurs, and apex predators including Tyrannosaurus rex occupied North American habitats. South America, Asia, Africa, and Europe each had their own faunas. The seas were ruled by mosasaurs and populated by plesiosaurs , ammonites, and fish. Pterosaurs still soared overhead, including the colossal Quetzalcoatlus . Mammals were small, mostly nocturnal, and ecologically marginal. Birds - avian dinosaurs - were diversifying. The world was warm, sea levels were high, and shallow epicontinental seas divided continents. Nothing in the fossil record suggests an imminent catastrophe.

title: Chicxulub
text: The Chicxulub impact The impact · ~66 Ma Approximately 66 million years ago, a rocky body roughly 10–15 kilometres across struck the shallow marine platform of what is now the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico at an estimated velocity of around 20 kilometres per second. The energy released was approximately one billion times that of the atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki combined. The target rocks were particularly consequential: thick deposits of limestone and gypsum - carbonates and sulfates - vaporised instantly, injecting enormous quantities of carbon dioxide and sulfur dioxide into the atmosphere alongside pulverised rock and superheated debris. The resulting crater, now known as Chicxulub, is approximately 180 kilometres in diameter and buried beneath the Yucatán and the Gulf of Mexico. The impactor itself was likely a carbonaceous chondrite or silicate asteroid. Its extraterrestrial origin is fingerprinted in the global abundance of iridium - rare in the Earth's crust but enriched at the K–Pg boundary clay layer found in rocks on every continent. Impact site: Yucatán Peninsula, Mexico. Crater name: Chicxulub. Crater diameter: ~180 km. Impactor size: ~10–15 km diameter. Age: 66.043 ± 0.011 Ma. Target rocks: Carbonates & sulfates.

title: Heat, waves, and falling sky
text: Minutes to days Minutes to days after impact The immediate aftermath was catastrophic on a regional and then global scale. The impact generated megatsunami that swept across the Gulf of Mexico and beyond. Earthquake-scale seismic waves propagated globally. Superheated ejecta - vaporised and pulverised rock - was thrown into ballistic trajectories and re-entered the atmosphere across the entire planet, briefly heating the upper atmosphere to temperatures that may have ignited wildfires across continents. Within hours, the sky was darkened by debris. Within days, a global layer of fine dust and sulfate aerosols began reducing sunlight reaching the surface. The initial thermal pulse may have been survivable in some habitats - underground, underwater, in burrows - but the disruption to the atmosphere had only just begun. The sulfur dioxide injected from the vaporised Yucatán gypsum beds converted to sulfate aerosols , which are particularly effective at blocking solar radiation and may have persisted in the stratosphere for years, driving what researchers now call the impact winter .

title: When the food chains broke
text: Months to years Months to years after impact The most destructive long-term consequence of the impact was not the blast, the heat, or the tsunamis. It was the interruption of photosynthesis. With sunlight reduced, land plants died back catastrophically. In the oceans, phytoplankton - the base of almost every marine food chain - collapsed. Darkness and cold spread globally. This is the food-web collapse cascade that drove the mass extinction. The impact itself likely killed few animals directly outside the immediate blast zone. What killed most species over the following months and years was starvation - the removal of the energy foundation that all food chains rested on. Animals with low energy requirements, those able to feed on detritus or seeds that persisted after plants died, and those with short generation times had advantages. Large-bodied animals dependent on continuous food supply - including most non-avian dinosaurs - did not. The impact winter persisted for months to years; full ecosystem recovery took millions of years. 01. Impact: Asteroid strikes carbonate- & sulfate-rich rocks on Yucatán at ~20 km/s 02. Ejecta & aerosols: Dust, soot, and sulfate aerosols reach the upper atmosphere globally within hours to days 03. Sunlight blocked: Photosynthesis drops sharply across land and ocean for months to years 04. Plants & plankton: Primary production at the base of every food chain collapses or crashes 05. Herbivores fail: Land and marine herbivores cannot find sufficient food 06. Predators follow: Carnivores lose prey; food webs unravel from the top down 07. Long recovery: Ecosystems rebuild over thousands to millions of years in the early Cenozoic

title: The losses
text: The K–Pg extinction Across the K–Pg boundary The end-Cretaceous extinction eliminated an estimated 75% of all species on Earth . The losses cut across every environment: terrestrial, marine, and freshwater. Among the most visible losses: all non-avian dinosaurs , all pterosaurs , all mosasaurs , all plesiosaurs , and all ammonites . In the sea, many species of fish, marine invertebrates, and reef-building organisms perished. On land, many lineages of lizards, snakes, and early mammals were eliminated alongside the dinosaurs. The K–Pg boundary - the rock layer marking the end of the Cretaceous Period and the beginning of the Paleogene - is one of the sharpest faunal transitions in the geological record. In many sections, the last fossils of non-avian dinosaurs lie immediately below a layer of clay enriched in iridium, above which no non-avian dinosaur fossils appear. The extinction was not instantaneous in every lineage, and some groups may have declined over a short interval following the impact. But the evidence overwhelmingly points to a geologically sudden event rather than a prolonged decline.

title: The survivors
text: After the boundary The K–Pg boundary and after Survival at the K–Pg boundary was not random, but neither was it predictable from any single trait. The groups that made it through shared certain characteristics: small body size , dietary flexibility, low metabolic requirements, or access to food sources that persisted through the impact winter. Some lineages of birds survived - these were avian dinosaurs, the only dinosaur lineage to cross the boundary. Every living bird is a direct descendant of dinosaurs that survived the end-Cretaceous extinction. Some mammals survived, setting the stage for the Cenozoic mammal radiation. Crocodyliforms , turtles, lizards, snakes, frogs, salamanders, and many freshwater invertebrates largely persisted. In the sea, sharks, bony fish, and many invertebrate groups survived. The pattern that emerges is one of selective extinction: the larger, more specialised, more food-dependent animals died; the smaller, more flexible, more tolerant ones lived. It was not a question of which animals were 'better' in any absolute sense - the survivors were simply better suited to the sudden and specific conditions of a world without reliable sunlight.

title: Dinosaurs after dinosaurs
text: Early Cenozoic Early Cenozoic · 66–60 Ma The early Cenozoic - the Paleogene Period - was not simply the world without dinosaurs. It was a world being rebuilt from the ground up. Ecosystems that had been shaped over tens of millions of years had to reassemble from survivors, many of whom had previously been ecologically marginal. Mammals, freed from ecological pressure from large non-avian dinosaurs, diversified explosively. Within a few million years of the boundary, the first large mammals appeared. Within ten million years, hoofed herbivores, early whales, bats, and primates were established. The Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum around 56 Ma further transformed ecosystems. Birds diversified into roles previously occupied by pterosaurs and small theropod dinosaurs: ground-dwelling predators, aerial insectivores, seed-eaters, and waterbirds. The avian radiation of the early Cenozoic produced most of the major modern bird lineages. The world the extinction made possible is the world we live in. Every large mammal, every songbird, every flowering meadow is part of an ecological order that began when the non-avian dinosaurs ended. Fossil Hunters Profile Luis & Walter Alvarez Proposed the impact hypothesis · Discovered the iridium anomaly · 1980

title: The evidence layer
text: How we know The K–Pg boundary in rock The impact hypothesis for the end-Cretaceous extinction was proposed in 1980 by physicist Luis Alvarez , his geologist son Walter Alvarez , and colleagues Frank Asaro and Helen Michel. They discovered that a thin clay layer at the K–Pg boundary in Gubbio, Italy, contained concentrations of iridium roughly thirty times higher than surrounding rocks. Iridium is rare in Earth's crust but common in certain types of asteroids. Subsequent research confirmed the iridium anomaly at hundreds of K–Pg boundary sites worldwide. Additional physical evidence accumulated: shocked quartz - quartz grains with microscopic planar deformation features that form only under extreme pressures, consistent with impact or nuclear explosion - was found globally at the boundary. Impact spherules , tiny glassy droplets of vaporised rock that solidified as they fell back through the atmosphere, were identified in boundary clay across the globe. The crater itself was identified in the late 1970s and confirmed in the 1990s by geophysical surveys and deep drilling on the Yucatán Peninsula. The Chicxulub crater, now buried beneath a kilometre of younger sediment, matches the age and scale predicted by the impact hypothesis precisely. Scientific consensus on the impact as the primary driver of the K–Pg extinction is strong. Iridium anomaly: Global enrichment at K–Pg boundary clay. Shocked quartz: Planar deformation features in quartz grains. Impact spherules: Glassy droplets from vaporised rock. Chicxulub crater: Confirmed by drilling and geophysics. Tsunami deposits: Disturbed sediments in Gulf region sites. Fossil turnover: Abrupt last occurrence of non-avian dinosaurs.

title: How a fossil forms
text: A fossil is any physical evidence of past life preserved in rock - a bone, a shell, a footprint, a burrow, a smear of carbon where a leaf once lay. The word comes from the Latin fossilis, meaning simply "dug up." But before anything can be dug up, an unlikely sequence of events has to unfold over millions of years - and at almost any point in that sequence, the evidence can be lost entirely. When an animal dies, soft tissue begins to decay within days. Without rapid burial, scavengers scatter the bones, soil acids dissolve what remains, and within decades almost nothing is left. The first requirement for fossilisation is speed: sediment - river silt, volcanic ash, a collapsing sand dune - must cover the remains quickly enough to protect them from the surface world. Once buried, a slower transformation begins. Sediment accumulates in layers above, and the weight compacts the material below. Mineral-rich groundwater percolates through the sediment and into the pore spaces of bone, gradually depositing minerals that fill and eventually replace the original tissue - a process called permineralisation. Over millions of years the bone becomes stone, chemically different from what it once was but retaining the original structure in extraordinary detail. The fossil now sits in bedrock, potentially kilometres below the surface. For it to ever be found, the rock has to come back up. Tectonic forces - the slow collision and separation of plates - uplift rock over millions of years, carrying the fossil closer to the surface. Erosion then does the rest: wind, rain, frost, and rivers strip away overlying rock grain by grain until a fragment of bone appears at a cliff face or eroded hillside. After perhaps a hundred million years underground, it is exposed to the surface world for the first time since burial. If nobody is looking, erosion continues and destroys it within decades. If someone is, that is where palaeontology begins.

title: Why the record is incomplete
text: The sequence above requires almost everything to go right. Rapid burial is the exception: most animals that die on land are scavenged, scattered, and dissolved before sediment can cover them. Even those that are buried face millions of years of chemical and geological processes that can destroy what was preserved. The species known through the fossil record are estimated to represent well under 1% of all species that have ever existed. That figure is worth sitting with. It is not a gap that more fieldwork will close. It is a structural property of how the record forms - and it is the starting point for everything else in this section.

title: Types of fossil
text: The popular image of a fossil is a bone turned to stone. That is one kind. Fossils also include compressions - organisms flattened to a thin film in fine-grained rock; moulds and casts, where original material dissolves and leaves only a shape in the surrounding sediment; unaltered remains preserved in amber or permafrost; and trace fossils - footprints, trackways, burrows, bite marks, eggs, and coprolites - which preserve not the animal itself but evidence of what it did. The distinction matters because different fossil types answer different questions. A trackway can preserve gait and, with some assumptions, walking speed - but tells you nothing about the animal's skull. An isolated tooth speaks to diet but almost nothing about body proportions. Knowing what kind of evidence you are holding is the first step towards knowing what you are entitled to conclude from it. The five main types: permineralised bone (minerals infiltrate the pore spaces in bone or wood, hardening it over millions of years - most dinosaur material is this type); compression (organism flattened to a thin film in fine-grained rock, common for plants and feathers, can preserve remarkable anatomical detail); mould and cast (original material dissolves, leaving a shaped cavity - if later filled with mineral, that infill is a cast of the original form); amber (tree resin traps and preserves small organisms in three dimensions, sometimes including soft tissue unmatched elsewhere); and trace fossils (a footprint, burrow, or coprolite - a record of behaviour, not the body itself, which can reveal locomotion and diet the skeleton cannot).

title: The record is biased, not balanced
text: Because fossilisation depends on specific conditions, the record is a skewed sample of past life rather than a fair one. Marine environments preserve better than terrestrial ones, because ocean sediments accumulate steadily where land surfaces erode. Hard parts outlast soft tissue - shells, bones, and teeth far outlast skin, muscle, and organs. Large, robust bones survive where small, delicate ones crumble. Floodplains and lake beds yield far more fossils than dry upland forests. And which regions palaeontologists have actually searched shapes the picture as much as what was once alive. The result is a record richest in large, hard-bodied animals from sedimentary lowland and marine settings, and poorest in small, soft-bodied organisms from dry uplands and under-sampled regions. Any honest reading of past life has to keep that filter in view.

title: How well any one species is known
text: The same unevenness applies at species level - and it directly shapes how much can be said about any given animal. Around half of all named valid dinosaur species are known from a single specimen; roughly four in five are based on incomplete skeletons. Tyrannosaurus rex sits at the well-documented end of the spectrum, represented by more than forty identified specimens, several of them substantially complete - enough to support confident discussion of growth rates, pathology, and ontogeny. Many species sit at the other end: one partial skeleton, a handful of fragments, or a single tooth. Almost everything that can be said about an animal's biology scales directly with where it falls on that spectrum. When entries in this guide differ in how much they say, that difference usually reflects the evidence, not the effort. The comparison spectrum used in this guide runs from Argentinosaurus (largest animal ever, known from a handful of vertebrae) through Megalosaurus (the first named dinosaur, still not fully known) and Velociraptor (a cultural icon, known from limited specimens) to Stegosaurus (multiple good specimens, well-studied) and Tyrannosaurus rex (40+ specimens, the most complete picture).

title: Windows of exceptional preservation
text: Occasionally conditions align to preserve far more than bone. Sites of exceptional preservation - Lagerstätten - act as windows onto what the ordinary record conceals, capturing soft tissue, fine anatomical detail, and sometimes entire communities that would otherwise be invisible to science. The Burgess Shale (~505 Ma, Middle Cambrian, British Columbia, Canada) preserves soft-bodied Cambrian marine animals - worms, arthropods, early chordates - that have almost no fossil record anywhere else. It revealed how much of early animal life left no ordinary trace. The Solnhofen Limestone (~150 Ma, Late Jurassic, Bavaria, Germany) is a fine-grained lagoonal limestone that preserved feather impressions and soft-tissue outlines. It is the source of Archaeopteryx, the most famous transitional fossil in the record. The Yixian Formation (~125 Ma, Early Cretaceous, Liaoning, China) formed from volcanic ash creating conditions fine enough to preserve feathered dinosaurs in microscopic detail, including pigment-bearing structures used to infer colour. It reshaped understanding of the dinosaur-bird transition. These sites matter not only for what they hold, but for what they imply about everything else. If the Burgess Shale shows a Cambrian sea full of soft-bodied life, it also reveals how much of every other Cambrian sea is missing from the record. Exceptional preservation is the exception that illuminates the rule.

title: Finding and Recovering Fossils
text: Palaeontology does most of its prospecting on foot, in places most people would not choose to spend time. Badlands, desert escarpments, eroding cliff faces, dry riverbeds - the landscapes that produce fossils are those where sedimentary rock is actively being stripped away and where bare ground means what erodes out stays visible. Fieldwork means slow walking across exposures, eyes down, looking for the colour and texture of bone among the debris.

title: Where fossils surface
text: The bone appearing at a hillside did not travel there. It was buried millions of years ago, and the rock around it has been removed grain by grain until the uppermost edge is exposed. Once exposed, erosion continues at the same rate, and a specimen that nobody finds within a few decades of first appearing may be entirely destroyed. The race between erosion and discovery is genuine. Sites are found through surface survey, satellite and drone imagery of promising terrain, and - more often than the field admits - local knowledge and chance. Farmers, ranchers, and construction workers have discovered some of the most significant specimens on record. Borealopelta, one of the best-preserved dinosaurs ever found, was spotted by a heavy equipment operator at an Alberta oil sands mine in 2011. The assumption that major discoveries come primarily from academic expeditions substantially understates the role of people who happen to be in the right landscape.

title: The commercial question
text: Not everyone who finds a fossil has an academic institution behind them, and where a fossil is found substantially determines what happens to it. In the United States, fossils on federal land are legally protected - they can only be collected under permit and must be permanently held in approved public institutions. Fossils found on private land, however, are the property of the landowner and may be sold commercially, with no access requirements and no obligation to allow research. The consequences surface periodically at auction. In 2020, a Tyrannosaurus rex known as Stan sold at Christie's for $31.8 million - later acquired for a museum in Abu Dhabi. In 2024, a Stegosaurus skeleton nicknamed Apex sold at Sotheby's for $44.6 million, the highest price ever paid for a fossil at auction. Palaeontologists are not simply irritated by the prices. When a specimen enters private hands, researcher access is not guaranteed, and studies based on privately held material are sometimes blocked from publication because owners can deny subsequent access needed to verify findings. A fossil kept from public institutions is, in an important sense, kept from science. The legal landscape varies considerably by country. In the UK there is no specific fossil legislation - finds on private land generally belong to the landowner, and there is no mandatory reporting system for significant specimens. Some European countries treat important fossils as cultural property with restrictions on export. China treats significant fossil material as state property. The patchwork of national laws means that where a fossil surfaces significantly shapes what becomes of it.

title: From site to lab
text: Once bone is identified at the surface, excavation begins - and this is where irreversible decisions are made. The spatial relationship between bones, and between bones and the surrounding sediment, is scientific data. The orientation of a limb, the position of a skull relative to the vertebrae, what else is present nearby - all inform how the animal died, how it was transported, and how complete the skeleton might be. Once excavation starts, that context is progressively destroyed. Good practice records everything before anything moves: photographs, GPS coordinates, scaled maps, sediment samples from the enclosing matrix. Bones are extracted still encased in surrounding rock, then wrapped in plaster field jackets - burlap strips soaked in plaster - for transport. In the laboratory, preparation removes that rock using air scribes, pneumatic chisels, dental picks, and chemical consolidants, under a microscope. A significant specimen can take months or years to prepare. The work is also partly interpretive: the boundary between bone and matrix is not always clear, and the decisions preparators make affect what the fossil appears to show. The pipeline from discovery to publication runs: discovery, survey and record, extraction, field jacket, preparation, CT and imaging, then analysis and publication.

title: Technology in the lab
text: CT scanning allows the interior of a rock block to be imaged without opening it - revealing bones still in matrix, internal skull anatomy, and growth structures that physical preparation might destroy or alter. For small or fragile specimens in particular, imaging first and preparing selectively afterwards has changed what is knowable without irreversible commitment. Photogrammetry builds accurate three-dimensional models from overlapping photographs, allowing specimens to be measured and studied remotely. This matters because physical access is a genuine bottleneck: researchers who cannot travel to the holding institution cannot fully engage with the material. Digital models extend access without replacing it. One consequence of improved collection over the last century is that museum storage has grown faster than the capacity to study what it contains. Major natural history institutions hold significant backlogs of uncatalogued and unprepared material. New findings sometimes come not from fieldwork but from re-examining specimens collected decades ago. The published record is a subset, not a complete inventory, of what has actually been recovered.

title: How We Know When
text: Working out when a fossil lived is a distinct problem from working out what it was. The two main categories of approach - relative dating and absolute dating - answer different questions and are most powerful when used together.

title: Sequence before numbers
text: The foundational approach is stratigraphy: in an undisturbed sequence of rock layers, lower layers are older than upper ones. This establishes the ordering of events without any numbers attached. It places animal A before animal B without saying how many years separate them. Complicating factors - unconformities where erosion removed a section of the sequence, faults that displaced layers, or folding that tilted the whole thing - mean that establishing the correct order is itself a skill requiring geological interpretation, not just observation. Biostratigraphy extends this principle geographically. Certain fossil species - index fossils - existed for only a short time geologically but were geographically widespread. Finding the same index fossil in two distant locations suggests those layers are approximately contemporaneous. The method was well-established by the early nineteenth century, long before radiometric dating was possible, and it remains a useful cross-check where other methods are unavailable.

title: Radiometric dating and the bracketing problem
text: Absolute dating assigns actual numbers of years by measuring the predictable decay of radioactive isotopes into stable daughter products. The central problem for palaeontology is that sedimentary rock - where fossils are found - cannot be reliably dated this way. Sedimentary rock is made of particles eroded from older material, so its constituent minerals could be millions of years older than the layer they now sit in. Measuring their isotope ratios would return the age of those source materials, not the moment of deposition. What can be dated is volcanic material - specifically the minerals that crystallise when magma or ash cools. When a mineral crystallises from cooling lava, gases including argon (a decay product of potassium) escape completely into the atmosphere. After crystallisation, any argon that accumulates in the mineral lattice must have come from radioactive decay since that moment. Measuring the ratio of potassium to argon gives the time elapsed since the mineral solidified - which is also the time since the ash fell or the lava erupted. The practical approach is bracketing: date a volcanic ash layer above the fossil-bearing rock and another below it. The fossil is younger than the upper date and older than the lower one. Its age is constrained between two independently dated events rather than measured directly. Different isotope systems suit different timescales. Argon-argon dating is well-suited to Mesozoic material - it can date volcanic rocks from hundreds of thousands to several billion years old with high precision. Uranium-lead dating, using much longer half-lives, is most useful for the oldest rocks on Earth. Radiocarbon dating, the best-known system to a general audience, works only on organic carbon and reaches back only around 50,000 years - entirely irrelevant for dinosaurs, which are at minimum 66 million years old.

title: Magnetostratigraphy
text: A third approach exploits a different physical record. Iron-bearing minerals in cooling lava, and in some fine-grained sediments, lock in the direction of Earth's magnetic field at the moment of formation. Earth's magnetic field has reversed polarity hundreds of times through geological history - north and south magnetic poles swapping - producing an irregular sequence of normal and reversed intervals. This sequence has been compiled into the Geomagnetic Polarity Timescale: a global record of reversals calibrated in years by radiometric dating. A local rock section can be sampled for its polarity pattern and that pattern matched against the timescale to establish age, much as a barcode is matched against a database. Magnetostratigraphy is particularly useful where volcanic ash is absent and direct radiometric dating is not possible. Because polarity reversals are geologically abrupt and globally simultaneous, they provide time markers that can be correlated across very different depositional environments. The method is most powerful when combined with biostratigraphic and radiometric constraints to confirm the pattern-match.

title: Convergence and uncertainty
text: The most reliable ages come when multiple independent methods agree. A fossil bracketed by two argon-argon dates, with biostratigraphic position consistent with both, and a magnetostratigraphy that places it in the right polarity interval, carries far more confidence than one constrained by a single method. When methods conflict, that is a signal to investigate further rather than to choose the most convenient answer. Every published age carries an uncertainty range, reflecting the precision of isotope measurements, the assumptions in the decay model, and the stratigraphic exactness of the bracketing. The "±" value attached to a date is not a weakness in the science - it is an honest statement of what the evidence can and cannot resolve. Understanding what those ranges mean, and resisting the urge to drop them for narrative convenience, is part of reading geochronological literature carefully.

title: Classification and Phylogenetics
text: The names given to extinct animals are not merely labels. They encode claims about evolutionary relationships, and when those claims are revised, the names change too. Understanding how classification works - and why it keeps changing - requires knowing something about the two systems it depends on: nomenclature, the rules for naming, and phylogenetics, the methods for establishing relationships.

title: The naming system
text: Every species in the scientific literature has a two-part Latin name - a binomial - consisting of a capitalised genus name and a lowercase species epithet, both italicised. Tyrannosaurus rex, Velociraptor mongoliensis, Triceratops horridus. The system was formalised by Carl Linnaeus in the eighteenth century and is now governed by the International Code of Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN). A key rule is priority: the first validly published name for a species takes precedence over all later ones. When two researchers independently name the same animal, the earlier name stands. When a name changes - as happens regularly in palaeontology - the animal itself has not changed. What has changed is the scientific understanding of its relationships. A species moved from one genus to another retains its species epithet but acquires a new full name. The creature is the same; the filing has been updated.

title: Holotypes and what happens when they are lost
text: Before a species is formally recognised, a researcher must publish a technical description anchored to a specific physical specimen: the holotype. This single specimen is permanently deposited in an accredited public institution and becomes the reference point to which all subsequent finds are compared. The holotype does not need to be complete, typical, or particularly well-preserved - its only function is nomenclatural. It is the permanent anchor of the name. What happens if the holotype is destroyed? The holotype of Spinosaurus aegyptiacus was held in Munich and lost in a wartime bombing in 1944. Without it, the name entered a prolonged state of uncertainty. The ICZN allows the designation of a replacement specimen - a neotype - under specific conditions, but this is a workaround that requires formal application and community consensus. The loss of original type material is a genuine scientific loss, not a technical inconvenience.

title: The species concept problem
text: In living animals, species can in principle be defined by reproductive isolation - populations that do not interbreed are different species. For extinct animals, reproductive behaviour is impossible to observe. Palaeontologists must define species on morphology alone: what the bones look like. This creates genuine ambiguity. Two specimens that look different might be the same species at different growth stages, different sexes, or from different geographic populations. Two that look similar might have been reproductively isolated for millions of years. The species concept in palaeontology is not a solved problem, and it underlies much of the taxonomic instability that gives the field its reputation for constant name-changing. The instability is real, but it reflects the difficulty of the evidence rather than carelessness in the science.

title: From classification to cladistics
text: Earlier approaches to classification grouped organisms by overall similarity. This produced groupings that sometimes reflected shared ancestry and sometimes did not. Dolphins, fish, and ichthyosaurs all share a streamlined body plan - none of that similarity indicates common descent. Modern palaeontology uses cladistics, developed by the German entomologist Willi Hennig in the mid-twentieth century and adopted widely in palaeontology from the 1980s onwards. Cladistics groups organisms by shared derived characteristics - features inherited from a specific common ancestor rather than arrived at independently. These shared derived features are called synapomorphies. A synapomorphy is a character state that appears in a group of organisms and marks their shared ancestry: it was present in their common ancestor and inherited by all descendants. The result is a cladogram: a branching diagram of hypothesised evolutionary relationships. A cladogram is not a proven family tree. It is a hypothesis built by coding observable characters across a set of taxa and finding the arrangement requiring the fewest evolutionary changes - the most parsimonious explanation. Different researchers scoring the same characters differently, or selecting different characters, can arrive at different trees. New specimens add new data to the matrix and the optimal tree may shift. This is not a weakness in the method. It is what a hypothesis-driven science looks like when it is working correctly.

title: Cladograms as revisable hypotheses
text: Any node in a cladogram could shift if new specimens added characters that changed the most parsimonious arrangement. That has happened repeatedly in dinosaur systematics - the controversial Baron et al. (2017) analysis moved ornithischians out of their traditional position and grouped them with theropods, which would require rewriting the family tree. The analysis remains contested. The family tree keeps getting redrawn not because palaeontologists are careless, but because the underlying data is genuinely ambiguous at some points and each new find adds to it. The Saurischia/Ornithischia split is itself defined by a physical feature - hip-bone arrangement - rather than pure genealogy, and that arrangement is a poor guide to bird ancestry despite the name "bird-hipped."

title: Reading Biology from Fossils
text: Reading biology from a fossil is an inferential process. The further a claim from what the bones directly show, the more cautiously it should be held. Palaeontology has developed a range of methods for extending beyond the skeleton, each with different confidence levels and different limits.

title: What the skeleton directly shows
text: The most direct observations come from the skeleton itself: anatomy and body proportions, the shape and articulation of skeletal elements, and pathology - healed fractures, bone infections, tumours, and arthritic changes that record injury, disease, and survival. The rough surfaces, ridges, and flanges where muscles once anchored are also directly readable: muscle attachment scars provide the raw data for reconstructing how an animal moved. From that data, biomechanists reconstruct locomotion, posture, and bite mechanics. Finite element analysis applies engineering stress calculations to digital models of bones and skulls, predicting how they respond to different forces and loads. These models are hypothesis-testing tools rather than measurements of behaviour: they tell you which movements were mechanically plausible, not which movements the animal actually used. Anatomy 101 walks through a case in point: the caudofemoralis longus, a hind-limb muscle read directly from scarring on fossil tail vertebrae.

title: Reading deeper into bone
text: Thin-sectioning a fossil bone reveals internal microstructure. The type of bone tissue and the pattern of growth marks known as Lines of Arrested Growth (LAGs) give estimates of age at death and growth rate. LAGs form annually in many vertebrates - analogously to tree rings - and counting them provides a minimum age for the individual. The spacing between LAGs indicates growth rate at each stage, and the overall bone tissue type carries implications for metabolic rate: the rapidly-deposited fibrolamellar bone seen in birds and mammals indicates fast growth consistent with high metabolism, while the slower lamellar bone typical of non-avian reptiles indicates slower growth. Most non-avian dinosaurs show fibrolamellar bone, a significant line of evidence in debates about dinosaur physiology. Stable isotope ratios in bone and tooth enamel encode information about diet and environment. Carbon isotopes distinguish different plant food types and trophic levels. Oxygen isotopes reflect body temperature regulation and the composition of water consumed, which varies geographically and seasonally - providing signals for migration and thermoregulation. Strontium isotopes carry a geological signature that differs between rock formations, giving independent evidence for movement across an animal's lifetime. Dental microwear - microscopic scratch and pit patterns on tooth surfaces - reflects what an animal ate in the weeks before death, as distinct from what its tooth shape was designed for. An herbivore's dentition might suggest browsing while microwear indicates unusually high grit content, pointing to low-growing abrasive plants or incidental soil ingestion. Diet inferred from tooth shape and diet inferred from microwear can diverge, and both are informative.

title: Phylogenetic bracketing
text: Many biological features do not survive fossilisation. For these, palaeontologists use phylogenetic bracketing - inferring likely features in an extinct animal by examining what its closest living relatives share. If a feature appears consistently in both sides of the living bracket around a fossil group, it can be inferred in that group with reasonable confidence, even without direct evidence. The canonical example is the four-chambered heart. No dinosaur heart has ever been preserved. But both birds - the living descendants of theropod dinosaurs - and crocodilians - the closest living relatives of dinosaurs outside the avian line - have four-chambered hearts. The inference that non-avian dinosaurs also had four-chambered hearts is therefore well-supported, not by direct fossil evidence but by the logic that such a fundamental cardiovascular structure would not have evolved independently in two such closely related lineages. The strength of a bracketing inference depends on how consistently the feature appears across both sides of the bracket. Features that are universal and stable in living bracket groups support stronger inferences than those that vary between sexes, ages, or populations. Bracketing is a method of reasoning under constrained uncertainty - a principled way of handling the absence of direct evidence, not a licence to assume extinct animals resembled modern ones in every detail.

title: Trace fossils and behaviour
text: Trace fossils - footprints, trackways, nesting sites, bonebeds, coprolites - provide evidence of behaviour that body fossils almost never can. A trackway records gait and can be used to estimate walking speed from stride length and inferred hip height. Multiple trackways of the same type moving in the same direction suggest possible gregarious behaviour, though mass-death assemblages without social structure can produce similar patterns. Nesting sites with eggs in structured arrangements and evidence of young remaining in the nest - as at the Maiasaura sites in Montana - imply active parental care. The primary limitation of trace fossils is identification. The animal that made a trackway can rarely be assigned to a species unless body fossils are found at the same site in the same rock unit. A three-toed theropod footprint could have been made by any of dozens of species. Ichnology - the study of trace fossils - operates at a different taxonomic resolution from body fossil palaeontology, and its behavioural inferences carry their own layers of uncertainty.

title: What each method can and cannot show
text: Bone histology (LAGs and tissue type) can show age at death, growth rate at each life stage, and tissue type as a metabolic indicator, but cannot show exact metabolic rate or soft tissue, and destroys material in the process. Stable isotopes (carbon, oxygen, strontium ratios in bone and enamel) can show trophic level, thermoregulation signals, and geographic movement across a lifetime, but cannot show specific food sources, exact migration routes, or the behavioural reason for movement. Dental microwear (microscopic tooth surface scratches) can show diet in the weeks before death and food hardness and grit content, but cannot show lifetime diet, soft or liquid foods that leave no scratch marks, or species-level identification. Biomechanics and finite element analysis (stress modelling of bones) can show which postures and forces were mechanically plausible and stress distribution in bone under load, but cannot show how the animal actually moved, exact muscle mass and attachment, or real-world behaviour. Trace fossils (tracks, nests, bonebeds, coprolites) can show gait, speed estimates, possible gregariousness, diet from coprolites, and evidence of nesting behaviour, but usually cannot show which species made a trackway, specific behavioural intent, or individual identity. Phylogenetic bracketing (inference from living relatives) can show the likely presence of soft features shared by both living bracket groups, such as a four-chambered heart, but cannot show the exact form of inferred features or features that vary across the bracket groups.

title: What fossils almost never tell us
text: Colour is almost always lost. A small number of exceptional specimens have preserved melanosomes - the microscopic pigment-producing organelles responsible for certain colours - allowing limited colour inference in those cases. But these are rare, and the reconstructions they support are partial: melanosome analysis identifies some colour patterns but not the full palette, and many colours depend on structural features that do not preserve. Sound and vocalisation are inferred at best from inner ear morphology and analogy with living relatives. Most behaviour is invisible in bone. The exact distribution of soft tissue - musculature, fat, skin texture, and features carried by organs - requires either exceptional preservation or inference from relatives, and carries degrees of uncertainty that popular accounts tend to understate. The principle applies throughout this guide. Every entry rests on a chain of inference from physical fossil evidence. The length of that chain is the measure of how cautiously each claim should be held - which is why evidence quality and confidence levels are noted where the inference runs longest.

title: From Interpretation to Knowledge
text: Publishing a new species description is the beginning of its scientific life, not the end. The process by which a preliminary observation becomes an accepted interpretation - and the conditions under which that acceptance can be withdrawn - is as important to understand as any of the methods described in the preceding sections.

title: Formal description and peer review
text: A new species is formally recognised through the publication of a technical description in a peer-reviewed journal. The description must identify the holotype, establish its repository, provide a diagnosis distinguishing the taxon from all known relatives, and justify the new name. The description is then reviewed by other experts in the field before publication - typically two or three, working anonymously, checking the methodology, the character scoring, and the conclusions. Peer review is an important quality control mechanism, but its limits in palaeontology are worth understanding. Reviewers assess manuscripts from descriptions and photographs; access to the physical specimen is not usually possible during review. Publication of a description is therefore the start of wider scrutiny rather than its conclusion. Subsequent researchers who access the specimen may find that the published description misses features, overstates their significance, or rests on a character scoring that does not hold up under re-examination. The role of specimen access in resolving disputes is substantial. Many disagreements in palaeontological literature persist precisely because researchers cannot readily examine the material on which competing claims rest. Fossils in well-resourced public institutions with open access policies generate more settled science than equally significant specimens held in private collections or in institutions that restrict study. The process is iterative rather than linear: specimen access, character coding, analysis, description writing, peer review, and publication feed into ongoing community scrutiny, which can prompt revision of any earlier step when new specimens, methods, or analyses challenge an existing interpretation.

title: Quantitative and statistical methods
text: From the 1970s onwards, a shift began in how palaeontology asks questions. Where the field had traditionally been largely descriptive - naming and classifying organisms, mapping their occurrence in the rock record - researchers began applying quantitative and statistical methods to questions about biodiversity, extinction rates, and evolutionary dynamics. This transformation has accelerated substantially since. Diversity curves - graphs of species richness through geological time - are a central output of this work. The fundamental problem is that raw counts of named species from any given time interval are heavily influenced by how much rock from that interval has been studied and how intensively. An interval that produced more sediment, or that happened to be excavated by more researchers, will appear more diverse than one that is geologically sparse or geographically remote, regardless of what was actually living then. Methods developed to correct for this include Shareholder Quorum Subsampling (SQS), which standardises species counts to comparable levels of sampling completeness rather than arbitrary sample size, allowing genuine patterns of diversity change to be separated from sampling artefacts. Bayesian methods have transformed phylogenetics by allowing uncertainty in tree topology to be quantified explicitly rather than resolved to a single best estimate. Instead of producing one most parsimonious tree, Bayesian analyses produce a distribution of trees consistent with the data, with probabilities attached. This is a more honest representation of what the evidence can and cannot determine. Body size trends through geological time, extinction rate calculations, and tests of evolutionary hypotheses all require statistical frameworks that are now standard tools in the field.

title: What it takes to overturn consensus
text: Overturning an established interpretation requires better evidence or a demonstrably stronger analysis of existing evidence - not just a new hypothesis. The history of palaeontology contains genuine paradigm shifts driven by both kinds of change. The feathered dinosaur discoveries from China, beginning with Sinosauropteryx formally described in 1996, represented a shift driven primarily by new material. The connection between non-avian dinosaurs and birds had been argued on anatomical grounds since Thomas Huxley in the nineteenth century and John Ostrom in the 1970s, but remained contested. The Yixian Formation specimens settled the question not through better argument but through direct physical evidence that could not reasonably be explained any other way. When the evidence is overwhelming and independently reproducible, even long-held alternatives tend to collapse. The cladistic revolution of the 1980s, by contrast, was a methodological shift. The underlying fossils had not changed. What changed was the analytical framework applied to them - from subjective grouping by overall similarity to rigorous analysis of shared derived characters. The same material, analysed differently, produced significantly different conclusions about evolutionary relationships. Both types of shift - new evidence and new methods - continue to operate in the field today.

title: The transformation of the field by technology
text: Palaeontology is not a Victorian discipline working with Victorian methods. From the 1980s onwards, technological transformation has substantially expanded what the fossil record can be made to yield.
